meta.title: A Fully Verified Proof of Sharp One-Sided Hardy Uniqueness

nodes.title: Sharp one-sided uniqueness
nodes.proofBody:
Lemma \ref{lem:gauge} removes the scalar \(c(t)\) without changing any
spatial decay class.  We may therefore assume
\begin{equation}\label{eq:tail-zero}
 \lim_{R\to\infty}\int_0^T
 \norm{V(\cdot,t)}_{L^\infty(\{x_1>R\})}\,dt=0.
\end{equation}

Lemma \ref{lem:appell} transforms the problem to an equation on
\([-1,1]\) whose two endpoint weights have the common exponent
\begin{equation}\label{eq:mu}
 \mu=\frac{T}{2}\sqrt{c_0c_1}.
\end{equation}
Condition \eqref{eq:threshold} is equivalent to \(\mu>1/8\).
Choose
\[
 \frac18<a<\mu.
\]
Theorem \ref{thm:stationary} gives the propagated bound
\eqref{eq:verified-stationary-bound}, and Theorem
\ref{thm:verified-iteration} gives
\[
 w(x,0)=0\quad\text{for almost every }x_1>0.
\]
Consequently
\[
 e^{Mx_{1,+}^2}w(0)\in L^2(\R^n)
 \quad\text{for every }M>0:
\]
the weight is identically one on \(\{x_1\le0\}\), while \(w(0)\) vanishes
on \(\{x_1>0\}\).  The propagated bound and \(q(r)=r^2\) for \(r\ge1\)
imply
\[
 e^{a x_{1,+}^2}w(1)\in L^2(\R^n).
\]
Lemma \ref{lem:product-halfspace}, applied on \([0,1]\) with \(A=M\)
and \(B=a\), gives \(w\equiv0\) once \(Ma>\Gamma^2\).  Such an \(M\)
can be chosen because it is arbitrary.  Every transformation in Lemmas
\ref{lem:gauge} and \ref{lem:appell} is invertible, hence \(u\equiv0\).

nodes.title: Gauge reduction
nodes.statementBody:
Under the assumptions of Theorem \ref{thm:conditional}, define
\[
 C(t)=\int_0^t c(r)\,dr,\qquad
 v(x,t)=e^{-iC(t)}u(x,t),\qquad
 W(x,t)=V(x,t)-c(t).
\]
Then \(v\in C([0,T];L^2)\) is a Duhamel solution of
\[
 \partial_tv=i(\Delta v+Wv),
\]
\(W\) satisfies \eqref{eq:tail-zero}, and, for every nonnegative
measurable spatial weight \(m(x)\),
\[
 mu(t)\in L^2\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad mv(t)\in L^2.
\]
nodes.proofBody:
Since \(c\in L^\infty(0,T)\), the function \(C\) is absolutely continuous.
For almost every \(t\),
\[
 \partial_t(e^{-iC}u)
 =-ic(t)e^{-iC}u+e^{-iC}\partial_tu
 =i\bigl(\Delta(e^{-iC}u)+(V-c)e^{-iC}u\bigr),
\]
because \(e^{-iC(t)}\) is independent of \(x\).  The same calculation in
integrated form proves the Duhamel identity without assuming a classical
time derivative.  Moreover,
\[
 |e^{-iC(t)}|=e^{\operatorname{Im}C(t)},\qquad
 e^{-T\norm{\operatorname{Im}c}_\infty}
 \le |e^{-iC(t)}|
 \le e^{T\norm{\operatorname{Im}c}_\infty}.
\]
Thus multiplication by the gauge factor is an invertible scalar operation
on every spatially weighted \(L^2\) space.  Finally,
\[
 \int_0^T\norm{W(\cdot,t)}_{L^\infty(\{x_1>R\})}\,dt
 =
 \int_0^T\norm{V(\cdot,t)-c(t)}_{L^\infty(\{x_1>R\})}\,dt,
\]
which proves the tail assertion.

nodes.title: One-sided Appell normalization
nodes.statementBody:
Assume \(c=0\) in \eqref{eq:tail-mod-scalar}.  There is an invertible
composition of a parabolic scaling, an Appell transformation, a time
translation, and a second parabolic scaling that maps \(u\) to a Duhamel
solution \(w\) on \(\R^n\times[-1,1]\) such that
\[
 e^{\mu x_{1,+}^2}w(-1),\qquad
 e^{\mu x_{1,+}^2}w(1)\in L^2(\R^n),
 \qquad
 \mu=\frac{T}{2}\sqrt{c_0c_1}.
\]
The transformed potential is bounded and has integrably vanishing right
tail.
nodes.proofBody:
First put
\[
 U(y,s)=T^{n/4}u(T^{1/2}y,Ts),\qquad 0\le s\le1.
\]
Then
\[
 \partial_sU=i(\Delta_yU+V_T(y,s)U),\qquad
 V_T(y,s)=T\,V(T^{1/2}y,Ts),
\]
and the endpoint exponents become \(Tc_0\) and \(Tc_1\).  Define
\[
 \beta=(Tc_0)^{-1/2},\qquad
 \alpha=(Tc_1)^{-1/2},
\]
\[
 \rho(\tau)=\alpha(1-\tau)+\beta\tau,\qquad
 s(\tau)=\frac{\beta\tau}{\rho(\tau)},\qquad
 y=\frac{\sqrt{\alpha\beta}}{\rho(\tau)}x.
\]
The Appell transform is
\begin{equation}\label{eq:appell}
 \widetilde U(x,\tau)=
 \left(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha\beta}}{\rho(\tau)}\right)^{n/2}
 U(y,s(\tau))
 \exp\left(\frac{(\alpha-\beta)|x|^2}
 {4i\rho(\tau)}\right).
\end{equation}
The phase in \eqref{eq:appell} has modulus one.  Direct differentiation
uses
\[
 s'(\tau)=\frac{\alpha\beta}{\rho(\tau)^2},
 \qquad
 \partial_\tau y=-\frac{\rho'(\tau)}{\rho(\tau)}y,
\]
and shows that the first-order terms from the dilation cancel those from
the quadratic phase.  Hence
\[
 \partial_\tau\widetilde U
 =i(\Delta_x\widetilde U+\widetilde V\widetilde U),
\]
where
\[
 \widetilde V(x,\tau)
 =\frac{\alpha\beta}{\rho(\tau)^2}
 V_T\left(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha\beta}}{\rho(\tau)}x,s(\tau)\right).
\]

For every \(\gamma>0\), the positive dilation in the \(x_1\)-variable and
the Jacobian in \eqref{eq:appell} give the exact norm identity
\begin{equation}\label{eq:weight-identity}
 \norm{e^{\gamma x_{1,+}^2}\widetilde U(\tau)}_2
 =
 \norm{
 e^{\gamma\rho(\tau)^2y_{1,+}^2/(\alpha\beta)}
 U(s(\tau))}_2.
\end{equation}
At \(\tau=0\) and \(\tau=1\), the two exponents on the right of
\eqref{eq:weight-identity} are respectively
\(\gamma\alpha/\beta\) and \(\gamma\beta/\alpha\).  Choosing
\(\gamma=(\alpha\beta)^{-1}\) gives
\[
 \frac{\gamma\alpha}{\beta}=\frac1{\beta^2}=Tc_0,
 \qquad
 \frac{\gamma\beta}{\alpha}=\frac1{\alpha^2}=Tc_1.
\]

Finally set
\[
 t=2\tau-1,\qquad z=\sqrt2\,x,\qquad
 w(z,t)=2^{-n/4}\widetilde U
 \left(\frac z{\sqrt2},\frac{t+1}{2}\right).
\]
Since \(\partial_t=\frac12\partial_\tau\) and
\(\Delta_x=2\Delta_z\), the coefficient of \(\Delta_z\) remains one.
The Gaussian exponent becomes
\[
 \mu=\frac{\gamma}{2}
 =\frac1{2\alpha\beta}
 =\frac{T}{2}\sqrt{c_0c_1}.
\]

It remains to check the tail.  The spatial dilation factor in the original
\(x_1\)-variable is
\[
 d(\tau)=\frac{T^{1/2}\sqrt{\alpha\beta}}{\sqrt2\,\rho(\tau)}.
\]
Because \(\rho\) is a positive affine function on \([0,1]\), there are
constants \(0<d_-\le d(\tau)\le d_+<\infty\).  After the changes
\(t=2\tau-1\) and \(r=Ts(\tau)\),
\begin{align*}
 &\int_{-1}^{1}
 \norm{\widehat V(\cdot,t)}
 _{L^\infty(\{z_1>R\})}\,dt\\
 &\qquad\le
 \int_0^T
 \norm{V(\cdot,r)}
 _{L^\infty(\{x_1>d_-R\})}\,dr
 \longrightarrow0.
\end{align*}
Boundedness follows from the positive lower bound for \(\rho\).

nodes.title: Verified weighted commutator identity
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(\phi=\phi(x_1,t)\) be a real \(C^4\) function in \(x_1\) and a
real \(C^2\) function in \(t\).  For \(f=e^\phi u\), define
\[
 S=-2i\phi_1\partial_{x_1}-i\phi_{11}+\phi_t,
 \qquad
 A=i\Delta+i\phi_1^2.
\]
Here a subscript \(1\) denotes an \(x_1\)-derivative.  On
\(C_c^\infty(\R^n)\), \(S\) is symmetric, \(A\) is skew-symmetric, and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:conjugated-operator}
 e^\phi(\partial_t-i\Delta)e^{-\phi}f
 =\partial_tf-Sf-Af.
\end{equation}
Moreover,
\begin{align}\label{eq:commutator-form}
 \langle(S_t+[S,A])f,f\rangle
={}&4\int_{\R^n}\phi_{11}|\partial_{x_1}f|^2\,dx
+4\int_{\R^n}\phi_{11}\phi_1^2|f|^2\,dx\notag\\
&-\int_{\R^n}\phi_{1111}|f|^2\,dx
+\int_{\R^n}\phi_{tt}|f|^2\,dx\notag\\
&+4\operatorname{Im}\int_{\R^n}
\phi_{1t}\partial_{x_1}f\,\overline f\,dx.
\end{align}
The transverse Laplacian \(\sum_{j=2}^n\partial_{x_j}^2\) contributes
nothing to the commutator.
nodes.proofBody:
The elementary identities
\[
 e^\phi\partial_{x_1}e^{-\phi}
 =\partial_{x_1}-\phi_1,
\]
\[
 e^\phi\partial_{x_1}^2e^{-\phi}
 =\partial_{x_1}^2-2\phi_1\partial_{x_1}
 +\phi_1^2-\phi_{11},
\]
and
\[
 e^\phi\partial_te^{-\phi}=\partial_t-\phi_t
\]
give \eqref{eq:conjugated-operator}.  The operator
\(\phi_1\partial_{x_1}+\frac12\phi_{11}\) is skew-symmetric on
\(C_c^\infty\); multiplication by a real function is symmetric.
It follows that \(S\) is symmetric.  The Laplacian and multiplication by
\(\phi_1^2\) are symmetric, so multiplication by \(i\) makes \(A\)
skew-symmetric.

Because the coefficients of \(S\) depend only on \(x_1,t\), every
\(\partial_{x_j}\), \(j\ge2\), commutes with \(S\).  It is therefore
enough to calculate in the \(x_1\)-variable.  Applying the product rule to
\(S_t+[S,A]\) gives the operator identity
\begin{align*}
 (S_t+[S,A])f
={}&-4\phi_{11}\partial_{x_1}^2f
 +(-4i\phi_{1t}-4\phi_{111})\partial_{x_1}f\\
&+\bigl(
 \phi_{tt}+4\phi_1^2\phi_{11}
 -2i\phi_{11t}-\phi_{1111}
 \bigr)f.
\end{align*}
We now take the \(L^2\) inner product with \(f\).  Integration by parts
gives
\begin{align*}
 -4\int\phi_{11}f_{11}\overline f
={}&4\int\phi_{11}|f_1|^2
 +4\int\phi_{111}f_1\overline f.
\end{align*}
The last term cancels the contribution
\(-4\int\phi_{111}f_1\overline f\).  To combine the remaining imaginary
terms, put
\[
 z=\int\phi_{1t}f_1\overline f,\qquad
 B=\int\phi_{11t}|f|^2.
\]
Integration by parts in \(x_1\) shows
\[
 2\operatorname{Re}z=-B.
\]
Consequently
\[
 -4iz-2iB=4\operatorname{Im}z.
\]
Substitution of these identities leaves exactly
\eqref{eq:commutator-form}.

nodes.title: Fourier commutators and signed transport
nodes.statementBody:
Use the Fourier transform
\[
 \widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\R^n}e^{-ix\cdot\xi}f(x)\,dx.
\]
Let \(P_\pm\) be the orthogonal Fourier projections with symbols
\(\one_{\{\pm\xi_1>0\}}\).  Let \(m\in C_c^\infty(\R^n)\), let
\(M_\varepsilon\) have symbol \(m(\varepsilon\xi)\), \(0<\varepsilon\le1\),
and put \(Q_{\varepsilon,\pm}=M_\varepsilon P_\pm\).

Suppose that \(a=a(x_1)\) is real and bounded, \(a'\in C_c^\infty(\R)\).
Then there is a constant \(C_m\), independent of \(\varepsilon\), such that
\begin{align}
 \norm{[Q_{\varepsilon,\pm},a]\partial_{x_1}f}_2
 +\norm{\partial_{x_1}[Q_{\varepsilon,\pm},a]f}_2
 \le C_m\norm{\widehat{a'}}_{L^1(\R)}\norm f_2 .
 \label{eq:fourier-commutator}
\end{align}
If, in addition, \(a\ge0\), then
\begin{align}
 \operatorname{Im}
 \langle Q_{\varepsilon,+}(a^2\partial_{x_1}f),
 Q_{\varepsilon,+}f\rangle
 &\ge -C_a\norm f_2^2, \label{eq:positive-transport}\\
 \operatorname{Im}
 \langle Q_{\varepsilon,-}(a^2\partial_{x_1}f),
 Q_{\varepsilon,-}f\rangle
 &\le C_a\norm f_2^2, \label{eq:negative-transport}
\end{align}
where \(\langle g,h\rangle=\int g\overline h\), and one may take
\[
 C_a=C_m\left(
 \norm a_\infty\norm{\widehat{a'}}_1+\norm{aa'}_\infty
 \right)\left(1+\norm a_\infty\right).
\]
For every real bounded \(b=b(x_1)\) with \(b'\in C_c^\infty(\R)\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:skew-transport}
 \left|\operatorname{Re}
 \langle Q_{\varepsilon,\pm}(b\partial_{x_1}f),
 Q_{\varepsilon,\pm}f\rangle\right|
 \le C_m\left(\norm{b'}_\infty+\norm{\widehat{b'}}_1\right)\norm f_2^2.
\end{equation}
Thus the constants needed for a fixed truncated weight are obtained without
invoking a general Calder\'on commutator theorem or pseudodifferential
calculus.
nodes.proofBody:
We first prove the two elementary multiplier estimates from which everything
else follows.  Since \(a\) depends only on \(x_1\), convolution by its Fourier
transform preserves the transverse frequency \(\xi'=(\xi_2,\ldots,\xi_n)\).
For the discontinuous symbol \(\chi_\pm(\xi_1)=\one_{\{\pm\xi_1>0\}}\),
\[
 \widehat{[P_\pm,a]\partial_{x_1}f}(\xi)
 =\frac1{2\pi}\int_\R
  \bigl(\chi_\pm(\xi_1)-\chi_\pm(\eta_1)\bigr)
  i\eta_1\,\widehat a(\xi_1-\eta_1)
  \widehat f(\eta_1,\xi')\,d\eta_1.
\]
The factor in parentheses vanishes unless \(\xi_1\) and \(\eta_1\) have
opposite signs.  On that set,
\[
 |\eta_1|\le|\xi_1-\eta_1|,\qquad
 |\xi_1|\le|\xi_1-\eta_1|.
\]
For \(\xi_1\ne\eta_1\),
\[
 |\xi_1-\eta_1|\,|\widehat a(\xi_1-\eta_1)|
 =|\widehat{a'}(\xi_1-\eta_1)|.
\]
The possible Dirac mass at zero in \(\widehat a\), caused by the two constant
limits of \(a\), is annihilated by the multiplier difference.  Young's
convolution inequality and Plancherel's theorem therefore give
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Ppm-commutators}
 \norm{[P_\pm,a]\partial_{x_1}f}_2+
 \norm{\partial_{x_1}[P_\pm,a]f}_2
 \le C\norm{\widehat{a'}}_1\norm f_2.
\end{equation}

Write \(\xi=(\xi_1,\xi')\), \(\eta=(\eta_1,\xi')\).  There is a constant
\(C_m\) such that
\begin{equation}\label{eq:smooth-symbol-difference}
 |m(\varepsilon\xi)-m(\varepsilon\eta)|
 \bigl(|\xi_1|+|\eta_1|\bigr)
 \le C_m|\xi_1-\eta_1|.
\end{equation}
Indeed, if
\(|\xi_1-\eta_1|\ge\frac12\max\{|\xi_1|,|\eta_1|\}\), boundedness of \(m\)
proves the claim.  Otherwise the two first coordinates are comparable.
The mean value theorem applies; if the multiplier difference is nonzero,
the segment meets the fixed compact support of a first derivative of \(m\),
so \(\varepsilon(|\xi_1|+|\eta_1|)\) is bounded.  This proves
\eqref{eq:smooth-symbol-difference}.  Repeating the preceding convolution
argument yields
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Meps-commutators}
 \norm{[M_\varepsilon,a]\partial_{x_1}f}_2+
 \norm{\partial_{x_1}[M_\varepsilon,a]f}_2
 \le C_m\norm{\widehat{a'}}_1\norm f_2.
\end{equation}
The identity
\[
 [M_\varepsilon P_\pm,a]
 =M_\varepsilon[P_\pm,a]+[M_\varepsilon,a]P_\pm
\]
and \eqref{eq:Ppm-commutators}--\eqref{eq:Meps-commutators} prove
\eqref{eq:fourier-commutator}.

It remains to prove the signs.  Put \(Q=Q_{\varepsilon,\pm}\).  The identity
\[
 a^2\partial_{x_1}f
 =a\partial_{x_1}(af)-aa'f
\]
gives
\[
 Q(a^2\partial_{x_1}f)
 =a\partial_{x_1}Q(af)
  +[Q,a]\partial_{x_1}(af)-Q(aa'f).
\]
Moreover,
\[
 aQf=Q(af)-[Q,a]f.
\]
Consequently, after moving the derivative in the term containing
\([Q,a]f\),
\begin{align*}
 \langle Q(a^2\partial_{x_1}f),Qf\rangle
 ={}&\langle\partial_{x_1}Q(af),Q(af)\rangle+E,\\
 |E|\le{}&C_m\left(
 \norm a_\infty\norm{\widehat{a'}}_1+\norm{aa'}_\infty
 \right)\left(1+\norm a_\infty\right)\norm f_2^2.
\end{align*}
Here \eqref{eq:fourier-commutator}, the \(L^2\)-boundedness of \(Q\), and
Cauchy--Schwarz were used; the calculation is first made for Schwartz
functions and then extended by density.

Finally, Plancherel's theorem gives
\[
 \operatorname{Im}
 \langle\partial_{x_1}Q(af),Q(af)\rangle
 =c_n\int_{\R^n}\xi_1
 |m(\varepsilon\xi)|^2\one_{\{\pm\xi_1>0\}}
 |\widehat{af}(\xi)|^2\,d\xi.
\]
This quantity is nonnegative for \(P_+\) and nonpositive for \(P_-\).
Combining this sign with the estimate for \(E\) proves
\eqref{eq:positive-transport} and \eqref{eq:negative-transport}.

For the last assertion, write \(g=Q_{\varepsilon,\pm}f\).  Then
\[
 Q_{\varepsilon,\pm}(b\partial_{x_1}f)
 =b\partial_{x_1}g+
 [Q_{\varepsilon,\pm},b]\partial_{x_1}f.
\]
Integration by parts gives
\[
 \operatorname{Re}\langle b\partial_{x_1}g,g\rangle
 =-\frac12\int_\R b'(x_1)|g(x)|^2\,dx.
\]
The second term is bounded by \eqref{eq:fourier-commutator}, with \(b\) in
place of \(a\).  This proves \eqref{eq:skew-transport}.

nodes.title: Fixed-slope linear exponential propagation
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(u\in C([0,1];L^2(\R^n))\) satisfy
\begin{equation}\label{eq:linear-schrodinger}
 i\partial_tu+\Delta u=Vu+H
\end{equation}
in the Duhamel sense, where
\[
 V\in L^1([0,1];L^\infty(\R^n)),\qquad
 H\in L^1([0,1];L^2(\R^n)).
\]
There is a dimensional number \(\varepsilon_n>0\) such that, if
\[
 \norm V_{L^1_tL^\infty_x}\le\varepsilon_n
\]
and, for one fixed \(\beta>0\),
\[
 e^{\beta x_1}u(0),\quad e^{\beta x_1}u(1)\in L^2,\qquad
 e^{\beta x_1}H\in L^1_tL^2_x,
\]
then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:linear-exponential-bound}
 \sup_{0\le t\le1}\norm{e^{\beta x_1}u(t)}_2
 \le C_n\left(
 \norm{e^{\beta x_1}u(0)}_2+
 \norm{e^{\beta x_1}u(1)}_2+
 \norm{e^{\beta x_1}H}_{L^1_tL^2_x}
 \right).
\end{equation}
The constant \(C_n\) is independent of \(\beta\).
nodes.proofBody:
Choose a real nonincreasing function \(\rho\in C^\infty(\R)\) such that
\[
 0\le\rho\le1,\qquad
 \rho(s)=1\ (s\le1),\qquad
 \rho(s)=0\ (s\ge10).
\]
For \(N\ge1\), define
\[
 d_N(x_1)=\beta\rho(x_1/N)^2,\qquad
 \theta_N(x_1)=\int_0^{x_1}d_N(s)\,ds.
\]
Then \(\theta_N\) is bounded above for fixed \(N\), so
\(e^{\theta_N}\) is a bounded multiplier, and
\[
 \theta_N(x_1)\le\beta x_1,\qquad
 \theta_N(x_1)\longrightarrow\beta x_1
 \quad(N\to\infty)
\]
for every \(x_1\).  Put
\[
 v_N=e^{\theta_N}u,\qquad c_N=d_N^2,\qquad
 w_N=e^{-itc_N}v_N.
\]
The multipliers \(e^{\theta_N}\) and \(e^{-itc_N}\) are bounded for fixed
\(N\), and the latter has modulus one.

We compute the equation rather than cite a conjugation formula.  Since
\[
 e^{\theta_N}\Delta e^{-\theta_N}v
 =\Delta v-2d_N\partial_{x_1}v+(d_N^2-d_N')v,
\]
equation \eqref{eq:linear-schrodinger} becomes
\[
 i\partial_tv_N+\Delta v_N
 =Vv_N+e^{\theta_N}H+
 2d_N\partial_{x_1}v_N+(d_N'-d_N^2)v_N.
\]
Using \(v_N=e^{itc_N}w_N\) and differentiating the phase gives
\begin{equation}\label{eq:truncated-weight-equation}
 i\partial_tw_N+\Delta w_N
 =Vw_N+G_N+a_N^2\partial_{x_1}w_N
 +itb_N\partial_{x_1}w_N+h_Nw_N,
\end{equation}
where
\begin{align*}
 G_N&=e^{-itc_N}e^{\theta_N}H,&
 a_N&=(2d_N)^{1/2},\\
 b_N&=-2c_N',&
 h_N&=d_N'+2itd_Nc_N'-itc_N''+t^2(c_N')^2.
\end{align*}
In particular, \(a_N\) is real and nonnegative, while \(b_N\) is real.

Every coefficient just displayed is a fixed smooth profile evaluated at
\(x_1/N\), multiplied by a power of \(\beta\) and, when differentiated,
the corresponding power of \(N^{-1}\).  Fourier scaling therefore gives,
for a finite constant \(K_\beta\) independent of \(N\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:scaled-coefficient-smallness}
 C_{a_N}+\norm{b_N'}_\infty+\norm{\widehat{b_N'}}_1+
 \norm{h_N}_\infty
 \le\frac{K_\beta}{N}.
\end{equation}
For example,
\[
 a_N'(x_1)=N^{-1}A_\beta'(x_1/N)
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 \norm{\widehat{a_N'}}_1
 =N^{-1}\norm{\widehat{A_\beta'}}_1,
\]
and every other term is identical or has an additional power of \(N^{-1}\).

Let \(m\in C_c^\infty(\R^n)\) be real, \(m=1\) near the origin, and use
\(Q_{\varepsilon,\pm}\) from Lemma \ref{lem:fourier-transport}.  Applying
it to \eqref{eq:truncated-weight-equation}, taking the \(L^2\) inner product
with \(Q_{\varepsilon,\pm}w_N\), and then taking imaginary parts gives
\begin{align}
 \frac{d}{dt}\norm{Q_{\varepsilon,+}w_N}_2^2
 &\ge-F_N(t),\label{eq:positive-energy}\\
 \frac{d}{dt}\norm{Q_{\varepsilon,-}w_N}_2^2
 &\le F_N(t),\label{eq:negative-energy}
\end{align}
where
\begin{equation}\label{eq:energy-error}
 F_N(t)\le C_n\left(
 \norm{V(t)}_\infty+\frac{K_\beta}{N}\right)\norm{w_N(t)}_2^2
 +C_n\norm{G_N(t)}_2\norm{w_N(t)}_2.
\end{equation}
Indeed, the Laplacian contributes zero because it is self-adjoint;
\eqref{eq:positive-transport}--\eqref{eq:negative-transport} give the
opposite signs for the \(a_N^2\partial_{x_1}\) term;
\eqref{eq:skew-transport} controls the real part generated by
\(itb_N\partial_{x_1}\); and the remaining terms follow from
Cauchy--Schwarz and \eqref{eq:scaled-coefficient-smallness}.

For completeness, these differential identities require no hidden spatial
regularity of \(u\).  The compactly supported multiplier
\(M_\varepsilon\) maps \(L^2\) into every spatial Sobolev space.  Thus the
projected equation is an absolutely continuous \(L^2\)-identity after a
time convolution.  The bounds in Lemma \ref{lem:fourier-transport} are
independent of the convolution scale, so its removal gives
\eqref{eq:positive-energy}--\eqref{eq:negative-energy} for almost every
time.

Let
\[
 S_N=\sup_{0\le t\le1}\norm{w_N(t)}_2
\]
and choose \(t_N\) where this maximum is attained.  Integrate
\eqref{eq:negative-energy} from \(0\) to \(t_N\), and integrate
\eqref{eq:positive-energy} backward from \(1\) to \(t_N\).  Add the two
inequalities and let \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\).  Since
\(M_\varepsilon\to I\) strongly on \(L^2\) and \(P_++P_-=I\), Plancherel's
theorem gives
\begin{align}
 S_N^2\le{}&
 \norm{w_N(0)}_2^2+\norm{w_N(1)}_2^2 \notag\\
 &+C_n\left(\norm V_{L^1_tL^\infty_x}
 +\frac{K_\beta}{N}\right)S_N^2
 +C_n\norm{G_N}_{L^1_tL^2_x}S_N.
\label{eq:maximal-truncated-bound}
\end{align}
Choose \(\varepsilon_n\) so that \(C_n\varepsilon_n\le1/4\), and then take
\(N\) sufficiently large that \(C_nK_\beta/N\le1/4\).  Absorption and
the elementary inequality \(AB\le\frac14A^2+B^2\) yield
\[
 S_N\le C_n\left(
 \norm{w_N(0)}_2+\norm{w_N(1)}_2+
 \norm{G_N}_{L^1_tL^2_x}\right).
\]
Because \(\theta_N\le\beta x_1\) and the phase has modulus one,
\[
 \norm{w_N(j)}_2\le\norm{e^{\beta x_1}u(j)}_2\quad(j=0,1),
 \qquad
 \norm{G_N}_{L^1_tL^2_x}
 \le\norm{e^{\beta x_1}H}_{L^1_tL^2_x}.
\]
The right side is independent of \(N\).  Finally,
\[
 |w_N(x,t)|=e^{\theta_N(x_1)}|u(x,t)|
 \longrightarrow e^{\beta x_1}|u(x,t)|
\]
pointwise.  Fatou's lemma, first at each \(t\) and then after taking the
supremum, proves \eqref{eq:linear-exponential-bound}.

nodes.title: Right-tail exponential propagation
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(u\in C([0,1];L^2(\R^n))\) solve
\[
 i\partial_tu+\Delta u=Vu
\]
in the Duhamel sense, where \(V\in L^\infty(\R^n\times[0,1])\) and
\[
 \lim_{R\to\infty}\int_0^1
 \norm{V(\cdot,t)}_{L^\infty(\{x_1>R\})}\,dt=0.
\]
If, for some fixed \(\beta>0\),
\[
 e^{\beta x_1}u(0),\qquad e^{\beta x_1}u(1)\in L^2(\R^n),
\]
then
\[
 \sup_{0\le t\le1}\norm{e^{\beta x_1}u(t)}_2<\infty.
\]
nodes.proofBody:
Choose \(R\) so large that
\[
 V_{\mathrm{tail}}=V\one_{\{x_1>R\}}
 \quad\text{satisfies}\quad
 \norm{V_{\mathrm{tail}}}_{L^1_tL^\infty_x}\le\varepsilon_n,
\]
where \(\varepsilon_n\) is from Lemma \ref{lem:linear-exponential}.  Write
\[
 i\partial_tu+\Delta u
 =V_{\mathrm{tail}}u+H_R,\qquad
 H_R=V\one_{\{x_1\le R\}}u.
\]
On the support of \(H_R\),
\[
 e^{\beta x_1}\le e^{\beta R}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
 \norm{e^{\beta x_1}H_R}_{L^1_tL^2_x}
 \le e^{\beta R}\norm V_\infty
 \norm u_{L^1_tL^2_x}
 \le e^{\beta R}\norm V_\infty
 \norm u_{C_tL^2_x}<\infty.
\]
Lemma \ref{lem:linear-exponential}, with \(V_{\mathrm{tail}}\) as the small
potential and \(H_R\) as the forcing term, proves the assertion.

nodes.title: Verified \(S/A\) logarithmic convexity
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(J=[c,d]\), let \(S(t)\) be symmetric and \(A(t)\) skew-symmetric on a
common dense domain in \(L^2(\R^n)\), and let \(f\) be a smooth
domain-valued function.  Put
\[
 H=\norm f_2^2,\qquad D=\langle Sf,f\rangle,\qquad
 E=\partial_tf-Sf-Af.
\]
Assume
\begin{equation}\label{eq:abstract-commutator-lower}
 \langle(S_t+[S,A])f,f\rangle\ge-\kappa H
\end{equation}
for some \(\kappa\ge0\).  For \(\varepsilon>0\), let \(M_\varepsilon\) solve
\[
 M_\varepsilon''(t)
 =-\frac{\norm{E(t)}_2^2}{H(t)+\varepsilon},\qquad
 M_\varepsilon(c)=M_\varepsilon(d)=0,
\]
and set
\[
 N_\varepsilon=\int_c^d
 \frac{|\operatorname{Re}\langle E(t),f(t)\rangle|}
 {H(t)+\varepsilon}\,dt,\qquad
 \vartheta(t)=\frac{d-t}{d-c}.
\]
Then
\begin{align}
 H(t)+\varepsilon
 \le{}&
 (H(c)+\varepsilon)^{\vartheta(t)}
 (H(d)+\varepsilon)^{1-\vartheta(t)}\notag\\
 &\times
 \exp\left\{\kappa(t-c)(d-t)+M_\varepsilon(t)
 +2N_\varepsilon\right\}.
\label{eq:abstract-logconv-output}
\end{align}
In particular, if \(E=iVf\) with
\(V\in L^\infty(J\times\R^n)\), then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:abstract-residual-bounds}
 0\le M_\varepsilon(t)
 \le\frac{(d-c)^2}{8}\norm V_\infty^2,\qquad
 N_\varepsilon\le(d-c)\norm V_\infty.
\end{equation}
nodes.proofBody:
All inner products below are defined on the common domain.  Symmetry of
\(S\) and skew-symmetry of \(A\) give
\begin{equation}\label{eq:H-prime}
 H'=2D+2e,\qquad e=\operatorname{Re}\langle E,f\rangle.
\end{equation}
Differentiating \(D\), substituting
\(\partial_tf=Sf+Af+E\), and moving \(S\) and \(A\) to the opposite side
of the inner product yields
\begin{equation}\label{eq:D-prime}
 D'=\langle(S_t+[S,A])f,f\rangle
 +2\norm{Sf}_2^2+2\operatorname{Re}\langle E,Sf\rangle.
\end{equation}

Put \(h=H+\varepsilon\).  From \eqref{eq:H-prime},
\[
 \partial_t\left((\log h)'-\frac{2e}{h}\right)
 =\partial_t\left(\frac{2D}{h}\right).
\]
Using \eqref{eq:D-prime} on the right gives
\begin{align*}
 \partial_t\left(\frac{2D}{h}\right)
 ={}&\frac{2\langle(S_t+[S,A])f,f\rangle}{h}\\
 &+\frac{4\norm{Sf}_2^2}{h}
 -\frac{4D^2}{h^2}
 +\frac{4\operatorname{Re}\langle E,Sf\rangle}{h}
 -\frac{4De}{h^2}.
\end{align*}
Let
\[
 g=Sf-\frac{D}{h}f.
\]
Since \(D^2\le H\norm{Sf}_2^2\) by Cauchy--Schwarz and \(H\le h\),
\[
 \frac{4\norm{Sf}_2^2}{h}-\frac{4D^2}{h^2}
 \ge\frac4h\norm g_2^2.
\]
The two terms containing \(E\) equal
\[
 \frac4h\operatorname{Re}\langle E,g\rangle.
\]
Completing the square,
\[
 \frac4h\left(\norm g_2^2+
 \operatorname{Re}\langle E,g\rangle\right)
 =\frac4h\norm{g+E/2}_2^2-\frac{\norm E_2^2}{h}
 \ge-\frac{\norm E_2^2}{h}.
\]
Together with \eqref{eq:abstract-commutator-lower}, this proves
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-log-differential}
 \partial_t\left((\log h)'-\frac{2e}{h}\right)
 \ge-2\kappa-\frac{\norm E_2^2}{h}.
\end{equation}

Set
\[
 T(t)=\kappa(t-c)(d-t),\qquad
 G(t)=\log h(t)-T(t)-M_\varepsilon(t).
\]
Because \(T''=-2\kappa\), equation
\eqref{eq:verified-log-differential} says that
\[
 G'(t)-\frac{2e(t)}{h(t)}
\quad\text{is nondecreasing}.
\]
For \(s\in[c,t]\) and \(r\in[t,d]\), compare this expression at \(s\)
and \(r\), then integrate over the rectangle
\([c,t]\times[t,d]\).  Division by \(d-c\) gives
\begin{align*}
 G(t)\le{}&
 \vartheta(t)G(c)+(1-\vartheta(t))G(d)\\
 &+2\vartheta(t)\int_c^t\frac{e(s)}{h(s)}\,ds
 -2(1-\vartheta(t))\int_t^d\frac{e(r)}{h(r)}\,dr.
\end{align*}
The last line is at most \(2N_\varepsilon\).  Since \(T\) and
\(M_\varepsilon\) vanish at \(c,d\), exponentiation proves
\eqref{eq:abstract-logconv-output}.

If \(E=iVf\), then
\[
 \frac{\norm E_2^2}{H+\varepsilon}\le\norm V_\infty^2,\qquad
 \frac{|\operatorname{Re}\langle E,f\rangle|}{H+\varepsilon}
 \le\norm V_\infty.
\]
The solution of \(-M''=q\), with zero endpoint data, is the Dirichlet
Green integral with nonnegative kernel.  Its integral against \(1\) is
\((t-c)(d-t)/2\le(d-c)^2/8\).  This proves
\eqref{eq:abstract-residual-bounds}.

nodes.title: Weighted-time \(S/A\) logarithmic convexity
nodes.proofBody:
Equations \eqref{eq:H-prime} and \eqref{eq:D-prime}, with
\(h=H+\varepsilon\) and \(e=\operatorname{Re}\langle E,f\rangle\), give
\[
 \partial_t\left[
 \gamma\left((\log h)'-\frac{2e}{h}\right)\right]
 =\partial_t\left(\frac{2\gamma D}{h}\right).
\]
Expanding the right side produces the commutator contribution
\[
 \frac{2}{h}
 \langle(\gamma S_t+\gamma[S,A]+\gamma'S)f,f\rangle.
\]
The remaining terms are exactly the square completed in the proof of
Lemma \ref{lem:SA-logconv}, multiplied by \(\gamma\), and are bounded
below by
\[
 -\gamma\frac{\norm E_2^2}{h}.
\]
Thus
\begin{equation}\label{eq:weighted-log-differential}
 \partial_t\left[
 \gamma\left((\log h)'-\frac{2e}{h}\right)\right]
 \ge-2\psi-\gamma\frac{\norm E_2^2}{h}.
\end{equation}
Set
\[
 G=\log h-2T-M_\varepsilon.
\]
Equation \eqref{eq:weighted-log-differential} says that the function
\[
 \gamma(t)\left(G'(t)-\frac{2e(t)}{h(t)}\right)
\]
is nondecreasing.  Compare its values at \(s\in[c,t]\) and
\(r\in[t,d]\).  Multiply the resulting inequality by
\(\gamma(s)^{-1}\gamma(r)^{-1}\), then integrate over
\([c,t]\times[t,d]\).  This gives
\[
 G(t)\le\Theta(t)G(c)+(1-\Theta(t))G(d)+2N_\varepsilon.
\]
Both Dirichlet solutions vanish at the endpoints.  Exponentiation proves
\eqref{eq:weighted-SA-output}.

nodes.title: Strict-loss weighted graph closure
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(J\subset\R\) be a bounded open interval and let
\[
 P=\partial_t-i\Delta-iV,\qquad
 V\in L^\infty(\R^n\times J).
\]
Let \(u\in C(J;L^2(\R^n))\) satisfy \(Pu=0\) in distributions.  Let
\(\Phi=\Phi(x_1,t)\ge0\) be smooth and suppose that, on every
\(J_0\Subset J\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:phase-growth}
 |\partial_t\Phi|+|\partial_{x_1}\Phi|
 \le C_{J_0}(1+|x_1|),\qquad
 c_{J_0}x_{1,+}-C_{J_0}
 \le\Phi(x_1,t)
 \le C_{J_0}(1+x_{1,+}^2)
\end{equation}
with \(c_{J_0}>0\).
Assume
\begin{equation}\label{eq:strong-weight-available}
 \sup_{t\in J}\norm{e^\Phi u(t)}_2<\infty.
\end{equation}
Then, for every \(0<\delta<1\) and \(J'\Subset J\), there are
\(u_j\in C_c^\infty(\R^n\times J)\) such that
\begin{align}
 e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}(u_j-u)&\longrightarrow0
 &&\text{in }C(J';L^2(\R^n)),\label{eq:graph-u}\\
 e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}Pu_j&\longrightarrow0
 &&\text{in }L^2(J'\times\R^n).\label{eq:graph-Pu}
\end{align}
nodes.proofBody:
We divide the proof into translation, mollification, and cutoff steps.

Let \(J_0\) satisfy \(J'\Subset J_0\Subset J\).  From
\eqref{eq:phase-growth} and the mean value theorem, for
\(z=(x,t)\), \(z'=(x',t')\), and \(|z-z'|\le\rho\le1\),
\[
 |\Phi(z)-\Phi(z')|
 \le C\rho(1+|x_1|+|x_1'|).
\]
If \(x_1'\) is large and positive, the linear lower bound in
\eqref{eq:phase-growth} absorbs the preceding right side after decreasing
\(\rho\).  If \(x_1'\) is bounded above, then \(\Phi(z)\) and
\(\Phi(z')\) are bounded above on the relevant left-hand region.  Hence,
after decreasing \(\rho\) if necessary,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:strict-translation}
 (1-\delta)\Phi(z)
 \le(1-\delta/2)\Phi(z')+C_{\delta,J_0}
 \qquad(|z-z'|\le\rho).
\end{equation}
This includes one-sided phases that are constant on the left and either
linear or quadratic on the active right tail.

Let \(\eta_\rho\) be a standard nonnegative space-time mollifier supported
in \(\{|z|<\rho\}\), and set \(u_\rho=\eta_\rho*u\) on \(J_0\).
Formula \eqref{eq:strict-translation} gives the pointwise domination
\begin{equation}\label{eq:mollifier-domination}
 e^{(1-\delta)\Phi(z)}|\eta_\rho*h(z)|
 \le C_{\delta,J_0}\,
 \eta_\rho*\bigl(e^{(1-\delta/2)\Phi}|h|\bigr)(z).
\end{equation}
Young's convolution inequality therefore bounds the left side in
\(L^2\) by the stronger weighted \(L^2\) norm of \(h\).

We next prove
\begin{equation}\label{eq:mollified-u-convergence}
 e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}(u_\rho-u)\to0
 \quad\text{in }C(J';L^2).
\end{equation}
On \(\{|x|\le R\}\), the weight is bounded and the conclusion follows
from the ordinary \(C_tL_x^2\) continuity of \(u\) and translation
continuity in \(L^2\).  On \(\{|x|>R\}\), split
\[
 e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}
 =e^{-\delta\Phi/2}e^{(1-\delta/2)\Phi}.
\]
The part with \(x_{1,+}\) large is uniformly small by
\eqref{eq:strong-weight-available} and the strict factor
\(e^{-\delta\Phi/2}\).  For bounded \(x_1\), the weight is bounded; the
set \(\{u(t):t\in\overline{J_0}\}\) is compact in \(L^2\), because
\(u\in C_tL_x^2\), and every compact subset of \(L^2\) has uniformly
small spatial tails.  Hence the part with bounded \(x_1\) and large
transverse variable is uniformly small.  Domination
\eqref{eq:mollifier-domination} gives the same tail bound for \(u_\rho\).
First let \(\rho\downarrow0\) on the compact part and then
\(R\uparrow\infty\), proving \eqref{eq:mollified-u-convergence}.

Because \(P\) has constant principal coefficients and \(Pu=0\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:potential-commutator}
 Pu_\rho
 =i\bigl(\eta_\rho*(Vu)-V(\eta_\rho*u)\bigr).
\end{equation}
This tends to zero locally in \(L^2(J'\times\R^n)\): indeed,
\(\eta_\rho*(Vu)\to Vu\) in local \(L^2\), while
\(\eta_\rho*u\to u\) in local \(L^2\), and multiplication by
\(V\in L^\infty\) is bounded on \(L^2\).  For the weighted tails,
\eqref{eq:mollifier-domination} gives
\[
 \norm{e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}\eta_\rho*(Vu)}_2
 \le C\norm{e^{(1-\delta/2)\Phi}Vu}_2,
\]
and
\[
 \norm{e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}V(\eta_\rho*u)}_2
 \le C\norm V_\infty
 \norm{e^{(1-\delta/2)\Phi}u}_2.
\]
After restricting both inequalities to the complement of a large compact
set, their right sides tend to zero uniformly for small \(\rho\), by
\eqref{eq:strong-weight-available} and the same translated-tail argument
used above.  Hence
\begin{equation}\label{eq:mollified-residual}
 e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}Pu_\rho\to0
 \quad\text{in }L^2(J'\times\R^n).
\end{equation}

It remains to make the approximants compactly supported.  Choose a
space-time cutoff \(\chi_R\in C_c^\infty(\R^n\times J_0)\) equal to one
on \(\{|x|\le R,\ t\in J'\}\), with
\[
 |\nabla_x\chi_R|\le C/R,\qquad
 |\Delta_x\chi_R|\le C/R^2,
\]
and put \(u_{\rho,R}=\chi_Ru_\rho\).  On \(J'\),
\[
 P(\chi_Ru_\rho)
 =\chi_RPu_\rho
 +(\partial_t\chi_R)u_\rho
 -i\bigl(2\nabla\chi_R\cdot\nabla u_\rho
 +(\Delta\chi_R)u_\rho\bigr).
\]
The time derivative vanishes on \(J'\).  For fixed \(\rho\), derivatives
of \(u_\rho\) are convolutions of \(u\) with derivatives of the mollifier;
the analogue of \eqref{eq:mollifier-domination} therefore puts
\(e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}\nabla u_\rho\) in \(L^2(J'\times\R^n)\).
The spatial cutoff errors are supported where \(|x|\simeq R\), so their
weighted \(L^2\) norms tend to zero as \(R\to\infty\).  The same tail
argument shows
\[
 e^{(1-\delta)\Phi}(\chi_Ru_\rho-u_\rho)\to0
 \quad\text{in }C(J';L^2).
\]
Choose first \(\rho_j\downarrow0\), using
\eqref{eq:mollified-u-convergence} and \eqref{eq:mollified-residual}, and
then \(R_j\uparrow\infty\) sufficiently rapidly.  The functions
\(u_j=u_{\rho_j,R_j}\) satisfy \eqref{eq:graph-u} and
\eqref{eq:graph-Pu}.

nodes.title: Verified stationary one-sided propagation
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(u\in C([-1,1];L^2(\R^n))\) satisfy
\[
 \partial_tu=i(\Delta u+Vu)
\]
in the Duhamel sense, where \(V\in L^\infty(\R^n\times[-1,1])\) has
integrably vanishing right tail.  Suppose that, for some \(\mu>0\),
\[
 e^{\mu x_{1,+}^2}u(-1),\qquad
 e^{\mu x_{1,+}^2}u(1)\in L^2(\R^n).
\]
There is a smooth convex \(q:\R\to[0,\infty)\) such that
\[
 q(r)=0\quad(r\le0),\qquad q(r)=r^2\quad(r\ge1),
\]
and, for every \(0<a<\mu\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-stationary-bound}
 \sup_{-1\le t\le1}\norm{e^{aq(x_1)}u(t)}_2<\infty.
\end{equation}
Moreover, for every compact interval \(I\Subset(-1,1)\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-stationary-graph}
 \int_I\int_{\{x_1>1\}}e^{2ax_1^2}
 \left(|\partial_{x_1}u|^2+x_1^2|u|^2\right)\,dx\,dt<\infty.
\end{equation}
nodes.proofBody:
We first construct \(q\).  Fix a smooth nondecreasing
\(\chi:\R\to[0,1]\), flat at \(0\) and \(1\), with
\(\chi=0\) on \((-\infty,0]\) and \(\chi=1\) on \([1,\infty)\).  For small
\(\delta>0\), put \(h_{0,\delta}(s)=2\chi(s/\delta)\) on \([0,1]\).
Thus \(h_{0,\delta}=0\) near \(0\), \(h_{0,\delta}=2\) away from a
shrinking neighborhood of \(0\), and
\[
 \int_0^1h_{0,\delta}\,ds\longrightarrow2,\qquad
 \int_0^1(1-s)h_{0,\delta}(s)\,ds\longrightarrow1.
\]
Choose nonnegative smooth bumps \(\psi_1,\psi_2\), supported in disjoint
small neighborhoods of two different points of \((1/3,2/3)\).  Their
moment vectors
\[
 \left(\int\psi_j,\int(1-s)\psi_j(s)\,ds\right),\qquad j=1,2,
\]
are linearly independent if the neighborhoods are sufficiently small,
because their limiting ratios are \(1-s_1\ne1-s_2\).  Hence there are
unique numbers \(\lambda_1(\delta),\lambda_2(\delta)\to0\) such that
\[
 h=h_{0,\delta}+\lambda_1(\delta)\psi_1+
 \lambda_2(\delta)\psi_2
\]
satisfies
\begin{equation}\label{eq:q-moments}
 \int_0^1h(s)\,ds=2,\qquad
 \int_0^1(1-s)h(s)\,ds=1.
\end{equation}
For small \(\delta\), the correction has absolute value less than \(1\)
where \(h_{0,\delta}=2\); hence \(h\ge0\).  Extend \(h\) by \(0\) to the
left and by \(2\) to the right, and define
\[
 q''=h,\qquad q(0)=q'(0)=0.
\]
Flatness at the junctions makes \(q\) smooth.  Convexity follows from
\(h\ge0\), and \eqref{eq:q-moments} gives
\[
 q'(1)=2,\qquad q(1)=1.
\]
Since \(q''=2\) for \(r\ge1\), this proves \(q(r)=r^2\) there.

We next truncate the curvature.  Let
\(\eta\in C^\infty(\R;[0,1])\) be nonincreasing, equal to \(1\) on
\((-\infty,1]\), and equal to \(0\) on \([2,\infty)\).  For \(R\ge2\),
define
\begin{equation}\label{eq:stationary-truncation}
 q_R''(r)=q''(r)\eta(r/R),\qquad q_R(0)=q_R'(0)=0.
\end{equation}
Then \(q_R=q\) on \((-\infty,R]\), \(q_R\) is convex and affine on
\([2R,\infty)\), and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:stationary-truncation-properties}
 0\le q_R\le q,\qquad q_R(r)\longrightarrow q(r),\qquad
 \sup_{R\ge2}\norm{q_R''''}_\infty<\infty.
\end{equation}
Indeed, the first assertion follows by integrating
\(0\le q_R''\le q''\) twice from zero.  For the last assertion,
\[
 q_R''''=q''''\eta+2R^{-1}q'''\eta'
 +R^{-2}q''\eta''.
\]
The first two terms vanish on \(R<r<2R\), because \(q''=2\) there, and
all remaining terms are uniformly bounded.

Fix \(0<a<a_1<\mu\).  For each fixed \(R\), write
\(q_R(r)=m_Rr+b_R\) for \(r\ge2R\), where \(m_R>0\).  The Gaussian
endpoint assumptions imply
\[
 e^{\beta x_1}u(-1),\ e^{\beta x_1}u(1)\in L^2
\quad\text{for every fixed }\beta>0,
\]
because \(\beta r\le\mu r_+^2+\beta^2/(4\mu)\).  Lemma
\ref{lem:tail-exponential}, after the affine change
\[
 \tau=(t+1)/2,\qquad y=x/\sqrt2,\qquad
 U(y,\tau)=2^{n/4}u(\sqrt2y,2\tau-1),
\]
with slope \(\sqrt2a_1m_R\) and transformed potential
\(-2V(\sqrt2y,2\tau-1)\), gives, together with the unweighted
\(C_tL^2_x\) bound,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:fixed-R-strong-weight}
 \sup_{[-1,1]}\norm{e^{a_1q_R(x_1)}u(t)}_2<\infty.
\end{equation}
Here the left half-line is handled by the unweighted norm, while on the
right \(a_1q_R(x_1)\le C_R+a_1m_Rx_1\).

For the phase \(\phi=aq_R\), Lemma \ref{lem:commutator} gives
\begin{align}
 \langle(S_t+[S,A])f,f\rangle
 ={}&4a\int q_R''|\partial_{x_1}f|^2\,dx
 +4a^3\int q_R''(q_R')^2|f|^2\,dx\notag\\
 &-a\int q_R''''|f|^2\,dx.
\label{eq:stationary-commutator-verified}
\end{align}
Thus
\[
 \langle(S_t+[S,A])f,f\rangle\ge-\kappa_a\norm f_2^2,
\qquad
 \kappa_a=a\sup_R\norm{q_R''''}_\infty,
\]
uniformly in \(R\).

The use of this identity for a Duhamel solution requires approximation.
In Lemma \ref{lem:graph-closure}, take
\(\Phi=a_1q_R\) and \(\delta=1-a/a_1\).  The phase is zero on the left,
affine with positive slope on the far right, and satisfies
\eqref{eq:phase-growth}.  For every \(-1<c<d<1\), the lemma supplies
compactly supported smooth \(u_j\) such that, with \(f_j=e^{aq_R}u_j\),
\[
 f_j\longrightarrow f_R:=e^{aq_R}u
 \quad\text{in }C([c,d];L^2),\qquad
 r_j=e^{aq_R}(\partial_t-i\Delta-iV)u_j\longrightarrow0
 \quad\text{in }L^2.
\]
Consequently
\[
 E_j=(\partial_t-S-A)f_j=iVf_j+r_j.
\]
Apply Lemma \ref{lem:SA-logconv} to \(f_j\), first with fixed
\(\varepsilon>0\).  Its estimates remain valid with the residual \(r_j\),
since
\[
 \frac{\norm{E_j}^2}{H_j+\varepsilon}
 \le2\norm V_\infty^2+2\varepsilon^{-1}\norm{r_j}^2,
\quad
 \frac{|\operatorname{Re}\langle E_j,f_j\rangle|}
 {H_j+\varepsilon}
 \le\norm V_\infty+\varepsilon^{-1/2}\norm{r_j}.
\]
Let \(j\to\infty\), then \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\).  We obtain
\begin{equation}\label{eq:stationary-interior-logconv}
 \norm{f_R(t)}_2^2
 \le C_{a,V}
 \norm{f_R(c)}_2^{2\vartheta(t)}
 \norm{f_R(d)}_2^{2(1-\vartheta(t))}
\end{equation}
for \(c\le t\le d\), where the constant is independent of \(R,c,d\) as
long as \(d-c\) stays between two fixed positive numbers.

We may now move \(c,d\) to the true endpoints.  For each fixed \(R\),
\eqref{eq:fixed-R-strong-weight} and unweighted continuity imply
\begin{equation}\label{eq:strict-weighted-trace}
 e^{aq_R}u(t)\longrightarrow e^{aq_R}u(t_0)
 \quad\text{in }L^2\quad(t\to t_0).
\end{equation}
To verify this, split at \(q_R=M\).  On \(\{q_R\le M\}\), use unweighted
continuity.  On \(\{q_R>M\}\), use
\[
 e^{2aq_R}\le e^{-2(a_1-a)M}e^{2a_1q_R}
\]
and \eqref{eq:fixed-R-strong-weight}; first choose \(M\) large and then
let \(t\to t_0\).  Apply \eqref{eq:stationary-interior-logconv} on
\([-1+h,1-h]\) and let \(h\downarrow0\).  Since \(q_R\le q\) and
\(q=x_{1,+}^2\) outside a compact interval,
\[
 \sup_R\norm{e^{aq_R}u(\pm1)}_2<\infty.
\]
We conclude that
\begin{equation}\label{eq:uniform-stationary-truncation}
 \sup_{R\ge2}\sup_{-1\le t\le1}
 \norm{e^{aq_R}u(t)}_2<\infty.
\end{equation}
Fatou's lemma and \(q_R\to q\) prove
\eqref{eq:verified-stationary-bound}.

It remains to retain the positive terms in
\eqref{eq:stationary-commutator-verified}.  Choose
\(a<a_1<\mu\), use \eqref{eq:verified-stationary-bound} at \(a_1\),
and apply Lemma \ref{lem:graph-closure} directly with
\(\Phi=a_1q\) and output weight \(aq\).  Thus there are compactly supported
smooth \(u_j\) for which \(f_j=e^{aq}u_j\to f=e^{aq}u\) and
\[
 E_j=(\partial_t-S-A)f_j=iVf_j+r_j,\qquad r_j\to0
\]
on every compact time interval.

For clarity, we derive the retained identity.  Equations
\eqref{eq:H-prime} and \eqref{eq:D-prime} give
\[
 D_j'=\langle(S_t+[S,A])f_j,f_j\rangle
 +2\norm{Sf_j}^2+2\operatorname{Re}\langle E_j,Sf_j\rangle.
\]
Completing the square yields
\[
 \langle(S_t+[S,A])f_j,f_j\rangle
 \le D_j'+\frac12\norm{E_j}^2.
\]
If \(\zeta\in C_c^\infty((-1,1))\) is nonnegative, then
\[
 -\int\zeta'D_j
 =\frac12\int\zeta''H_j+\int\zeta'
 \operatorname{Re}\langle E_j,f_j\rangle.
\]
Therefore
\begin{equation}\label{eq:retained-commutator-verified}
 \int\zeta\langle(S_t+[S,A])f_j,f_j\rangle\,dt
 \le C_\zeta\int\left(H_j+\norm{E_j}^2\right)\,dt.
\end{equation}
The right side is bounded as \(j\to\infty\), because
\(f_j\to f\) in \(C_tL^2_x\) on the support of \(\zeta\) and
\(E_j=iVf_j+r_j\).

Insert \eqref{eq:stationary-commutator-verified}, now with \(q_R=q\),
into \eqref{eq:retained-commutator-verified}.  The term involving
\(q''''\) is bounded by \(C\int H_j\).  Since \(q''\ge0\), the resulting
uniform derivative bound, weak compactness in \(L^2\), and strong
convergence \(f_j\to f\) identify the weak limit with the distributional
derivative \(\partial_{x_1}f\).  Weak lower semicontinuity, together with
Fatou's lemma for the zero-order term, gives, on every
\(I\Subset(-1,1)\),
\[
 \int_I\int q''|\partial_{x_1}f|^2+
 q''(q')^2|f|^2\,dx\,dt<\infty.
\]
On \(\{x_1>1\}\), \(q=x_1^2\), \(q'=2x_1\), and \(q''=2\).  Finally,
\[
 e^{2aq}|\partial_{x_1}u|^2
 =|\partial_{x_1}f-aq'f|^2
 \le2|\partial_{x_1}f|^2+2a^2(q')^2|f|^2.
\]
This proves \eqref{eq:verified-stationary-graph}.

nodes.title: Verified algebraic Gaussian recursion
nodes.statementBody:
For a positive smooth function \(a\), put \(c=a^{-1/2}\) and
\[
 F(a)=\frac1a\left(a''-\frac{3(a')^2}{2a}+32a^3\right).
\]
Then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:F-c-verified}
 F(a)=\frac2c\left(16c^{-3}-c''\right).
\end{equation}

Fix \(\mu>0\).  Suppose \(a_k=c_k^{-2}\) is positive and even on
\([-1,1]\), \(a_k(\pm1)=\mu\), \(a_k\) is nonincreasing on \([0,1]\),
and \(F(a_k)>0\).  Let \(b_k\) be the unique solution
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-b-ode}
 b_k''=-2c_k(16c_k^{-3}-c_k''),\qquad b_k(\pm1)=0.
\end{equation}
Then \(b_k\) is even, strictly positive on \((-1,1)\), and nonincreasing
on \([0,1]\).  If
\[
 1-a_kb_k>0
\]
and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-recursion}
 a_{k+1}=\frac{a_k}{1-a_kb_k},\qquad
 c_{k+1}=(c_k^2-b_k)^{1/2},
\end{equation}
then \(a_{k+1}\) has all the same structural properties.  Moreover,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-c-update}
 c_{k+1}''
 =c_{k+1}^{-3}\left(
 16-\frac{(b_k')^2}{4}+c_kc_k'b_k'
 -(c_k')^2b_k-16c_k^{-2}b_k\right)
 <16c_{k+1}^{-3},
\end{equation}
and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-weighted-identity}
 \frac1\mu=c_{k+1}(0)^2+
 \int_0^1(1-t)\left(32a_k(t)+2(c_k'(t))^2\right)\,dt.
\end{equation}

Starting with \(a_1\equiv\mu\), iterate while the denominator is positive.
If the iteration is infinite and \(\sup_k a_k(0)<\infty\), then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-threshold-conclusion}
 \mu\le\frac18.
\end{equation}
nodes.proofBody:
Direct differentiation of \(a=c^{-2}\) gives
\[
 a'=-2c^{-3}c',\qquad
 a''=6c^{-4}(c')^2-2c^{-3}c'',
\]
and substitution proves \eqref{eq:F-c-verified}.

Because the right side of \eqref{eq:verified-b-ode} is even, uniqueness
gives that \(b_k\) is even.  Equation \eqref{eq:F-c-verified} and
\(F(a_k)>0\) show that \(b_k''<0\).  Thus \(b_k\) is strictly concave;
with zero endpoint values it is positive inside.  Evenness gives
\(b_k'(0)=0\), so concavity gives \(b_k'\le0\) on \([0,1]\).

The two expressions in \eqref{eq:verified-recursion} are equivalent,
because
\[
 c_{k+1}^2=a_{k+1}^{-1}
 =a_k^{-1}-b_k=c_k^2-b_k.
\]
They show that \(a_{k+1}\ge a_k\), that \(a_{k+1}\) is even, and that its
endpoint value is \(\mu\).  Differentiating
\(c_{k+1}^2=c_k^2-b_k\) gives
\[
 c_{k+1}c_{k+1}'=c_kc_k'-\frac12b_k'.
\]
Since \(c_k'\ge0\) and \(b_k'\le0\) on \([0,1]\), one has
\(c_{k+1}'\ge0\); hence \(a_{k+1}\) is nonincreasing there.

Write \(d=c_{k+1}\) and
\[
 N=c_kc_k'-\frac12b_k'=dd'.
\]
Using \eqref{eq:verified-b-ode},
\[
 N'=(c_k')^2+c_kc_k''-\frac12b_k''
 =(c_k')^2+16c_k^{-2}.
\]
Therefore
\begin{align*}
 d''
 &=\frac{N'd^2-N^2}{d^3}\\
 &=d^{-3}\left(
 16-\frac{(b_k')^2}{4}+c_kc_k'b_k'
 -(c_k')^2b_k-16c_k^{-2}b_k\right).
\end{align*}
On \((0,1)\), every term after \(16\) is nonpositive, and at least the
last one is strictly negative.  This proves
\eqref{eq:verified-c-update}.  Formula \eqref{eq:F-c-verified} then gives
\(F(a_{k+1})>0\).

We next prove the integral identity.  Equation
\eqref{eq:verified-b-ode} can be rewritten as
\[
 b_k''=(c_k^2)''
 -\left(32c_k^{-2}+2(c_k')^2\right).
\]
Thus, for \(g_k=c_k^2-b_k=c_{k+1}^2\),
\[
 g_k''=32c_k^{-2}+2(c_k')^2,\qquad g_k'(0)=0.
\]
Since \(g_k(1)=c_k(1)^2=\mu^{-1}\), two integrations from zero give
\eqref{eq:verified-weighted-identity}.

It remains to prove the threshold conclusion.  At every continuing stage,
\(a_{k+1}\ge a_k\), so \(c_{k+1}\le c_k\).  If \(a_k(0)\) is bounded,
then the \(c_k\) stay uniformly bounded above and away from zero.
Furthermore \(c_k\) is nondecreasing on \([0,1]\), and
\[
 0\le c_k'(t)
 =\int_0^tc_k''(s)\,ds
 <16\int_0^tc_k(s)^{-3}\,ds.
\]
Hence the family is uniformly Lipschitz.  Its pointwise decreasing limit
\(c\) is positive, and Arzel\`a--Ascoli shows that the convergence is
uniform.  Therefore
\[
 b_k=c_k^2-c_{k+1}^2\longrightarrow0
\quad\text{uniformly}.
\]

Integrating the rewritten \(b_k''\) from zero gives
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-b-prime}
 b_k'(t)=
 -\int_0^t\left(32c_k^{-2}+2(c_k')^2\right)\,ds
 +2c_k(t)c_k'(t).
\end{equation}
This bounds \(b_k'\) uniformly.  Formula
\eqref{eq:verified-c-update} then bounds \(c_{k+1}''\) uniformly.
After passage to a subsequence,
\[
 c_k\longrightarrow c\quad\text{in }C^1([-1,1]).
\]

For \(g_k=c_{k+1}^2\), the identity for \(g_k''\), integrated from
\(t\) to \(1\), is
\[
 b_k(t)+\mu^{-1}
 =c_k(t)^2+
 \int_t^1\int_0^s
 \left(32c_k(\tau)^{-2}+2(c_k'(\tau))^2\right)\,d\tau\,ds.
\]
Letting \(k\to\infty\) gives
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-limit-integral}
 \mu^{-1}
 =c(t)^2+
 \int_t^1\int_0^s
 \left(32c(\tau)^{-2}+2(c'(\tau))^2\right)\,d\tau\,ds.
\end{equation}
Differentiating twice yields
\[
 c''=16c^{-3},\qquad c'(0)=0,\qquad c(1)=\mu^{-1/2}.
\]

Let \(c(0)^2=4/R\), \(R>0\).  Multiplying the ODE by \(c'\) gives the
first integral
\[
 (c')^2=16\left(c(0)^{-2}-c^{-2}\right),
\]
from which direct differentiation verifies the unique solution
\[
 c(t)^2=\frac4R(1+R^2t^2).
\]
Consequently
\[
 \mu=c(1)^{-2}=\frac{R}{4(1+R^2)}
 \le\frac18,
\]
because \(2R\le1+R^2\).  This proves
\eqref{eq:verified-threshold-conclusion}.

nodes.title: Verified moving one-sided Gaussian completion
nodes.statementBody:
There is a smooth convex \(Q:\R\to[0,\infty)\) such that
\[
 Q(y)=0\quad(y\le0),\qquad
 Q(y)=y^2-C_Q\quad(y\ge1),
\]
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Q-ratio}
 0\le\frac{Q'(y)}y\le2
\end{equation}
with the quotient extended by zero at \(y=0\), and
\[
 D_Q(y)=4Q''(y)(Q'(y))^2-Q''''(y)-16yQ'(y)
\]
is supported in \([0,1]\).

Let \(a,b\) be smooth real functions on an interval, \(a>0\), let
\(\xi\ge0\), and put
\[
 y=\sqrt a\,(x_1+b\xi),\qquad
 \phi(x_1,t)=Q(y),\qquad \gamma=a^{-1}.
\]
For the operators \(S,A\) of Lemma \ref{lem:commutator}, every compactly
supported smooth \(f\) satisfies
\begin{align}
 &\langle(\gamma S_t+\gamma[S,A]+\gamma'S)f,f\rangle\notag\\
 ={}&
 4\int Q''(y)
 \left|-i\partial_{x_1}f+\frac{y_t}{2\sqrt a}f\right|^2\,dx\notag\\
 &+\int\left[
 aD_Q(y)+\frac{F(a)}{2a}yQ'(y)
 +\frac{b''\xi}{\sqrt a}Q'(y)\right]|f|^2\,dx.
\label{eq:moving-gaussian-completion}
\end{align}
Consequently, if \(F(a)>0\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:moving-gaussian-lower}
 \langle(\gamma S_t+\gamma[S,A]+\gamma'S)f,f\rangle
 \ge-\left(
 \frac{|b''|^2}{F(a)}\xi^2+K_Qa\right)\norm f_2^2
\end{equation}
for a constant \(K_Q\) depending only on \(Q\).

Assume additionally that the interval is \([-1,1]\), \(a,b\) are even,
\(a\) is nonincreasing on \([0,1]\), and, with \(c=a^{-1/2}\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:moving-special-b}
 b''=-2c(16c^{-3}-c''),\qquad b(\pm1)=0.
\end{equation}
If \(T\) is the zero-boundary solution
\begin{equation}\label{eq:moving-T}
 (a^{-1}T')'=-\frac{|b''|^2}{F(a)},
\end{equation}
then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:T-less-b}
 0\le T(t)\le b(t)\qquad(-1\le t\le1).
\end{equation}
nodes.proofBody:
Choose the same smooth step \(\chi\) used in Theorem
\ref{thm:stationary}, and define
\[
 Q'(y)=2y\chi(y),\qquad Q(0)=0.
\]
Then
\[
 Q''(y)=2\chi(y)+2y\chi'(y)\ge0,
\]
which proves convexity and \eqref{eq:Q-ratio}.  For \(y\ge1\),
\[
 Q(y)=y^2-C_Q,\qquad
 C_Q=\int_0^12s(1-\chi(s))\,ds.
\]
All derivatives entering \(D_Q\) agree with those of \(y^2-C_Q\) for
\(y\ge1\), and vanish for \(y\le0\); hence \(D_Q\) is supported in
\([0,1]\).  In particular \(D_Q\ge-K_Q\).

We verify \eqref{eq:moving-gaussian-completion}.  Put
\[
 p=\frac{a'}a,\qquad h=y_t=\frac p2y+\sqrt a\,b'\xi.
\]
The required chain-rule derivatives are
\begin{align*}
 \phi_1&=\sqrt a\,Q',&
 \phi_{11}&=aQ'',&
 \phi_{1111}&=a^2Q'''',\\
 \phi_t&=Q'h,&
 \phi_{1t}&=\sqrt a\left(\frac p2Q'+Q''h\right),&
 \phi_{tt}&=Q''h^2+Q'y_{tt},
\end{align*}
where
\[
 y_{tt}=
 \left(\frac{a''}{2a}-\frac{(a')^2}{4a^2}\right)y
 +\sqrt a\left(b''+\frac{a'}a b'\right)\xi.
\]
Insert these expressions into the quadratic form
\eqref{eq:commutator-form}.  Since \(\gamma'=-p/a\), symmetry of \(S\)
and one integration by parts give
\begin{align*}
 \langle\gamma'Sf,f\rangle
 ={}&-\frac{2p}{\sqrt a}
 \int Q'\operatorname{Im}(\partial_{x_1}f\,\overline f)\,dx
 -\frac p a\int Q'h|f|^2\,dx.
\end{align*}
The mixed term contributed by \(\gamma(S_t+[S,A])\) is
\[
 \frac4{\sqrt a}\int
 \left(\frac p2Q'+Q''h\right)
 \operatorname{Im}(\partial_{x_1}f\,\overline f)\,dx.
\]
Thus its \(pQ'\)-part cancels the first term from \(\gamma'S\).
Moreover,
\begin{align*}
 \frac1a Q'y_{tt}-\frac p aQ'h
 ={}&
 \left(\frac{a''}{2a^2}
 -\frac{3(a')^2}{4a^3}\right)yQ'
 +\frac{b''\xi}{\sqrt a}Q',
\end{align*}
so the \(pb'\xi Q'\)-terms cancel as well.  The remaining derivative,
\(h^2\), and mixed terms are
\[
 4Q''|\partial_{x_1}f|^2+
 \frac{Q''h^2}{a}|f|^2+
 \frac{4Q''h}{\sqrt a}
 \operatorname{Im}(\partial_{x_1}f\,\overline f),
\]
which is the first square in
\eqref{eq:moving-gaussian-completion}.  The remaining zero-order
coefficient is
\[
 a\left(4Q''(Q')^2-Q''''\right)
 +\left(
 \frac{a''}{2a^2}-\frac{3(a')^2}{4a^3}\right)yQ'
 +\frac{b''\xi}{\sqrt a}Q'.
\]
Adding and subtracting \(16ayQ'\), and using
\[
 \frac{F(a)}{2a}
 =\frac{a''}{2a^2}-\frac{3(a')^2}{4a^3}+16a,
\]
proves \eqref{eq:moving-gaussian-completion}.

Set \(z(y)=Q'(y)/y\).  By \eqref{eq:Q-ratio}, \(0\le z\le2\).  Completing
the scalar square gives
\begin{align*}
 &z\left(\frac{F(a)}{2a}y^2+
 \frac{b''\xi}{\sqrt a}y\right)\\
 &\qquad\ge-\frac{z|b''|^2}{2F(a)}\xi^2
 \ge-\frac{|b''|^2}{F(a)}\xi^2.
\end{align*}
Together with \(aD_Q\ge-K_Qa\), this proves
\eqref{eq:moving-gaussian-lower}.

It remains to prove \eqref{eq:T-less-b}.  Formula
\eqref{eq:F-c-verified} and \eqref{eq:moving-special-b} give
\[
 \frac{|b''|^2}{F(a)}
 =2c^3(16c^{-3}-c'').
\]
Evenness and uniqueness imply \(T'(0)=b'(0)=0\).  Solving
\eqref{eq:moving-T} on \([0,1]\), with \(T(1)=0\), gives
\[
 T(t)=2\int_t^1\int_0^s
 \frac{a(s)}{a(\tau)}c(\tau)
 (16c(\tau)^{-3}-c''(\tau))\,d\tau\,ds.
\]
Since \(a\) is nonincreasing,
\[
 \frac{a(s)}{a(\tau)}\le1
 \qquad(0\le\tau\le s\le1).
\]
On the other hand, integrating \eqref{eq:moving-special-b} with
\(b'(0)=0\) and \(b(1)=0\) gives
\[
 b(t)=2\int_t^1\int_0^s
 c(\tau)(16c(\tau)^{-3}-c''(\tau))\,d\tau\,ds.
\]
The integrands are nonnegative because \(F(a)>0\).  Hence
\(0\le T\le b\) on \([0,1]\), and evenness proves
\eqref{eq:T-less-b} on the whole interval.

nodes.title: Verified shifted one-sided Gaussian estimate
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(u\in C([-1,1];L^2(\R^n))\) be a Duhamel solution of
\[
 \partial_tu=i(\Delta u+Vu),\qquad
 V\in L^\infty(\R^n\times[-1,1]).
\]
Let \(a,b\in C^\infty([-1,1])\) be even, let \(a>0\) be
nonincreasing on \([0,1]\), and assume
\[
 a(\pm1)=\mu,\qquad b(\pm1)=0,\qquad F(a)>0.
\]
Suppose
\begin{equation}\label{eq:shifted-strict-invariant}
 \sup_{-1\le t\le1}
 \norm{e^{(a(t)-\eta)x_{1,+}^2}u(t)}_2<\infty
 \quad\text{for every }0<\eta<\min a,
\end{equation}
and put
\[
 E_\mu=
 \norm{e^{\mu x_{1,+}^2}u(-1)}_2+
 \norm{e^{\mu x_{1,+}^2}u(1)}_2.
\]
Assume \(E_\mu<\infty\).
Let \(T\) be the zero-boundary solution
\begin{equation}\label{eq:shifted-T}
 (a^{-1}T')'=-\frac{|b''|^2}{F(a)}.
\end{equation}
Then, for every \(\xi\ge0\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-shifted-estimate}
 \norm{e^{a(t)(x_1+b(t)\xi)_+^2}u(t)}_2^2
 \le C_*e^{2T(t)\xi^2},\qquad -1\le t\le1,
\end{equation}
where \(C_*\) is independent of \(\xi\), of \(\max a\), and of all
derivatives of \(a,b\).  It may be chosen as a function only of
\[
 E_\mu,\quad \norm V_\infty,\quad Q,\quad
 \int_0^1a(s)\,ds.
\]
nodes.proofBody:
Write \(r=x_1+b(t)\xi\).  For \(0<\lambda<1\), sufficiently close to one,
put
\[
 a_\lambda=\lambda a,\qquad
 \phi_\lambda(x_1,t)=Q(\sqrt{a_\lambda(t)}\,r).
\]
Positivity of \(F(a)\) on the compact interval and continuity in
\(\lambda\) imply \(F(a_\lambda)>0\) for all sufficiently large
\(\lambda<1\).

We first verify that the formal weighted calculation is available.  For
every \(\kappa>0\),
\[
 (x_1+b\xi)_+^2
 \le(1+\kappa)x_{1,+}^2+
 (1+\kappa^{-1})\norm b_\infty^2\xi^2.
\]
Choose \(\kappa\) so that \(\lambda(1+\kappa)<1\).  Since
\(Q(y)\le y_+^2\), there are \(\eta_\lambda>0\) and
\(C_{\lambda,\xi}<\infty\) such that
\begin{equation}\label{eq:shifted-phase-available}
 \phi_\lambda(x_1,t)
 \le(a(t)-\eta_\lambda)x_{1,+}^2+C_{\lambda,\xi}.
\end{equation}
Thus \eqref{eq:shifted-strict-invariant} supplies the stronger weighted
norm needed for each fixed \(\xi\).

To obtain graph approximants at the exact phase \(\phi_\lambda\), choose
\(\lambda<\lambda'<1\) and \(\sigma>0\) so that
\(\lambda<(1-\sigma)\lambda'\).  Since \(Q(y)=y^2-C_Q\) for \(y\ge1\)
and both sides vanish for \(r\le0\), compactness of the transition region
gives
\begin{equation}\label{eq:two-scale-Q-gap}
 \phi_\lambda\le(1-\sigma)\phi_{\lambda'}+C_{\lambda,\lambda',Q}.
\end{equation}
The phase \(\phi_{\lambda'}\) satisfies the growth hypotheses of Lemma
\ref{lem:graph-closure}, and \eqref{eq:shifted-phase-available} holds with
\(\lambda'\).  Lemma \ref{lem:graph-closure}, followed by
\eqref{eq:two-scale-Q-gap}, therefore gives compactly supported smooth
approximants for the phase \(\phi_\lambda\) on every compact subinterval
of \((-1,1)\).

Apply Lemma \ref{lem:moving-gaussian} with \(a_\lambda\) and define
\(T_\lambda,R_\lambda\) by
\begin{align}
 (a_\lambda^{-1}T_\lambda')'
 &=-\frac{|b''|^2}{F(a_\lambda)},&
 T_\lambda(\pm1)&=0,\label{eq:T-lambda}\\
 (a_\lambda^{-1}R_\lambda')'
 &=-K_Qa_\lambda,&
 R_\lambda(\pm1)&=0.\label{eq:R-lambda}
\end{align}
The weighted commutator lower bound is then
\[
 \langle(a_\lambda^{-1}S_t+
 a_\lambda^{-1}[S,A]+(a_\lambda^{-1})'S)f,f\rangle
 \ge-\left(
 \frac{|b''|^2}{F(a_\lambda)}\xi^2+K_Qa_\lambda
 \right)\norm f_2^2.
\]

We record uniform bounds for every non-parameter error in the weighted
logarithmic-convexity lemma.  Evenness of \(a_\lambda\) and
\eqref{eq:R-lambda} give
\begin{align}
 0\le R_\lambda(t)\le R_\lambda(0)
 &=K_Q\int_0^1a_\lambda(s)
 \int_0^sa_\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\,ds\notag\\
 &=\frac{K_Q}{2}
 \left(\int_0^1a_\lambda(s)\,ds\right)^2.
\label{eq:transition-green-bound}
\end{align}
For the potential term, let \(\overline M_\lambda\) be the zero-boundary
solution
\[
 (a_\lambda^{-1}\overline M_\lambda')'
 =-a_\lambda^{-1}\norm V_\infty^2.
\]
Its forcing and coefficient are even, so
\(\overline M_\lambda'(0)=0\).  Hence
\begin{align}
 0\le\overline M_\lambda(t)
 \le\overline M_\lambda(0)
 &=\norm V_\infty^2
 \int_0^1a_\lambda(s)
 \int_0^sa_\lambda(\tau)^{-1}\,d\tau\,ds\notag\\
 &\le\frac12\norm V_\infty^2.
\label{eq:potential-green-bound}
\end{align}
The last inequality uses
\(a_\lambda(s)\le a_\lambda(\tau)\) for
\(0\le\tau\le s\le1\).  The one-dimensional comparison principle shows
that the Dirichlet error generated by
\[
 \frac{\norm{iVf}_2^2}{\norm f_2^2+\varepsilon}
 \le\norm V_\infty^2
\]
is bounded by \(\overline M_\lambda\).  Moreover,
\[
 \int_{-1}^1
 \frac{|\operatorname{Re}\langle iVf,f\rangle|}
 {\norm f_2^2+\varepsilon}\,dt
 \le2\norm V_\infty.
\]

Apply Lemma \ref{lem:weighted-SA-logconv} to the compactly supported
approximants on \([-1+h,1-h]\).  Their graph residuals are handled exactly
as in the proof of Theorem \ref{thm:stationary}: first keep the
regularizing denominator \(\varepsilon>0\), pass to the graph limit, and
then let \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\).  Estimates
\eqref{eq:transition-green-bound} and
\eqref{eq:potential-green-bound} yield
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Q-shifted-interior}
 \norm{e^{\phi_\lambda(t)}u(t)}_2^2
 \le C_0
 \exp\left\{
 2T_\lambda(t)\xi^2+
 K_Q\left(\int_0^1a(s)\,ds\right)^2
 \right\},
\end{equation}
where \(C_0\) depends only on \(E_\mu\) and \(\norm V_\infty\).

For completeness, the endpoint passage in this argument does not assume
weighted continuity.  Choose \(\lambda<\lambda'<1\).  On a fixed compact
set, ordinary \(L^2\)-continuity and uniform convergence of the phases give
\[
 e^{\phi_\lambda(t)}u(t)\longrightarrow
 e^{\phi_\lambda(t_0)}u(t_0)
 \quad\text{in }L^2
\]
as \(t\to t_0\).  On its complement,
\eqref{eq:two-scale-Q-gap} and the uniform
\(e^{\phi_{\lambda'}}u\)-bound make the tail uniformly small.  Thus the
same convergence holds globally.  At \(t_0=\pm1\), \(b(t_0)=0\) and
\[
 \phi_\lambda(x_1,t_0)
 \le\lambda\mu x_{1,+}^2\le\mu x_{1,+}^2,
\]
so the endpoint factors in Lemma \ref{lem:weighted-SA-logconv} are bounded
by \(E_\mu^2\).  This justifies letting \(h\downarrow0\) in
\eqref{eq:Q-shifted-interior}.

As \(\lambda\uparrow1\), the coefficients and right sides in
\eqref{eq:T-lambda} converge uniformly to those in
\eqref{eq:shifted-T}; hence \(T_\lambda\to T\) uniformly.  Fatou's lemma
gives
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Q-shifted-final}
 \norm{e^{Q(\sqrt a\,r)}u(t)}_2^2
 \le C_0
 \exp\left\{
 2T(t)\xi^2+
 K_Q\left(\int_0^1a(s)\,ds\right)^2
 \right\}.
\end{equation}
Finally,
\[
 B_Q:=\sup_{y\in\R}\bigl(y_+^2-Q(y)\bigr)<\infty,
\qquad
 a(t)r_+^2\le Q(\sqrt a\,r)+B_Q.
\]
Multiplying the right side of \eqref{eq:Q-shifted-final} by \(e^{2B_Q}\)
proves \eqref{eq:verified-shifted-estimate} with the asserted dependence.

nodes.title: Verified sharp one-sided iteration
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(q\) be the weight constructed in Theorem \ref{thm:stationary}.  Let
\(u\in C([-1,1];L^2(\R^n))\) be a Duhamel solution of
\[
 \partial_tu=i(\Delta u+Vu),\qquad
 V\in L^\infty(\R^n\times[-1,1]).
\]
If \(\mu>1/8\) and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:iteration-input}
 \sup_{-1\le t\le1}\norm{e^{\mu q(x_1)}u(t)}_2<\infty,
\end{equation}
then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:iteration-conclusion}
 u(x,0)=0\quad\text{for almost every }x\in\{x_1>0\}.
\end{equation}
nodes.proofBody:
Because \(q=0\) on \((-\infty,0]\), \(q(r)=r^2\) for \(r\ge1\), and \(q\)
is continuous,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:q-plus-equivalence}
 B_q:=\sup_{r\in\R}|q(r)-r_+^2|<\infty.
\end{equation}
Thus \eqref{eq:iteration-input} implies
\[
 \sup_{[-1,1]}\norm{e^{\mu x_{1,+}^2}u(t)}_2<\infty.
\]
In particular, the endpoint quantity \(E_\mu\) in Proposition
\ref{prop:shifted-gaussian} is finite.

Start the recursion of Lemma \ref{lem:algebraic-recursion} with
\[
 a_1\equiv\mu,\qquad c_k=a_k^{-1/2},
\]
and, at each stage, let \(b_k\) solve
\begin{equation}\label{eq:iteration-b}
 b_k''=-2c_k(16c_k^{-3}-c_k''),\qquad b_k(\pm1)=0.
\end{equation}
As proved in that lemma, \(a_k,b_k\) are even, \(a_k(\pm1)=\mu\),
\(a_k\) and \(b_k\) are nonincreasing on \([0,1]\), \(b_k>0\) in the
interior, and \(F(a_k)>0\).

We prove inductively the strict stage invariant
\begin{equation}\label{eq:verified-stage-invariant}
 \sup_{[-1,1]}
 \norm{e^{(a_k(t)-\eta)x_{1,+}^2}u(t)}_2<\infty
 \quad\text{for every }0<\eta<\min a_k.
\end{equation}
For \(k=1\), this follows from \eqref{eq:q-plus-equivalence} and
\eqref{eq:iteration-input}.  Assume it at stage \(k\).  Proposition
\ref{prop:shifted-gaussian} and Lemma \ref{lem:moving-gaussian} give
\begin{equation}\label{eq:stage-shifted}
 \norm{e^{a_k(t)(x_1+b_k(t)\xi)_+^2}u(t)}_2^2
 \le C_k e^{2b_k(t)\xi^2},\qquad \xi\ge0.
\end{equation}

We first treat a possible denominator obstruction.  Since both factors are
nonincreasing on \([0,1]\), \(a_kb_k\) is nonincreasing there.  Hence, if
\(1-a_kb_k\) is not positive everywhere, then
\[
 a_k(0)b_k(0)\ge1.
\]
At \(t=0\), multiply the integrand in \eqref{eq:stage-shifted} by
\(e^{-2b_k(0)\xi^2}\).  For every fixed \(x_1>0\), its exponent is
\begin{align*}
 &2a_k(0)(x_1+b_k(0)\xi)^2-2b_k(0)\xi^2\\
 &\quad=2a_k(0)x_1^2+4a_k(0)b_k(0)x_1\xi
 +2b_k(0)(a_k(0)b_k(0)-1)\xi^2,
\end{align*}
which tends to \(+\infty\) as \(\xi\to\infty\).  Fatou's lemma applied to
\[
 \int_{\R^n}
 e^{2a_k(0)(x_1+b_k(0)\xi)_+^2-2b_k(0)\xi^2}
 |u(x,0)|^2\,dx\le C_k
\]
forces \(u(x,0)=0\) for almost every \(x_1>0\), proving
\eqref{eq:iteration-conclusion}.

We may therefore suppose
\begin{equation}\label{eq:positive-denominator}
 1-a_k(t)b_k(t)>0\qquad(-1\le t\le1)
\end{equation}
and define
\[
 a_{k+1}=\frac{a_k}{1-a_kb_k}.
\]
The weighted identity \eqref{eq:verified-weighted-identity} gives
\begin{equation}\label{eq:weighted-a-bound}
 \int_0^1(1-t)a_k(t)\,dt\le\frac1{32\mu}.
\end{equation}
Since \(a_k\) and \(1-t\) are nonnegative and nonincreasing, their
Chebyshev integral inequality gives
\[
 \int_0^1(1-t)a_k(t)\,dt
 \ge\left(\int_0^1(1-t)\,dt\right)
 \left(\int_0^1a_k(t)\,dt\right)
 =\frac12\int_0^1a_k(t)\,dt.
\]
For reference, this inequality follows directly by integrating the
nonnegative function
\[
 (a_k(s)-a_k(t))((1-s)-(1-t))
\]
over \([0,1]^2\).  Consequently,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:uniform-a-L1}
 \int_0^1a_k(t)\,dt\le\frac1{16\mu}.
\end{equation}
Proposition \ref{prop:shifted-gaussian} therefore permits one common
constant \(C_*\), independent of \(k\), in \eqref{eq:stage-shifted} at
every continuing stage.  In particular, setting \(\xi=0\) yields
\begin{equation}\label{eq:uniform-exact-stage}
 \sup_k\sup_{[-1,1]}
 \norm{e^{a_k(t)x_{1,+}^2}u(t)}_2^2\le C_*,
\end{equation}
where the supremum is over all stages for which the recursion continues.

It remains to propagate the strict invariant to \(k+1\).  Fix
\(\varepsilon>0\).  Multiply \eqref{eq:stage-shifted} by
\[
 e^{-2(1+\varepsilon)b_k(t)\xi^2}
\]
and integrate over \(\xi\ge0\).
At an interior time abbreviate
\[
 a=a_k(t),\qquad b=b_k(t),\qquad
 D=b(1+\varepsilon-ab)>0.
\]
For \(x_1>0\), completion of the scalar square gives
\begin{align}
 &\int_0^\infty
 e^{2a(x_1+b\xi)^2-2(1+\varepsilon)b\xi^2}\,d\xi\notag\\
 &\quad=
 e^{2A_{k,\varepsilon}(t)x_1^2}
 \int_0^\infty
 e^{-2D\left(
 \xi-\frac{ax_1}{1+\varepsilon-ab}\right)^2}\,d\xi\notag\\
 &\quad\ge
 \frac{\sqrt\pi}{2\sqrt{2D}}\,
 e^{2A_{k,\varepsilon}(t)x_1^2},
\label{eq:half-line-lower}
\end{align}
where the Gaussian center is nonnegative and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:strict-next-coefficient}
 A_{k,\varepsilon}
 =\frac{(1+\varepsilon)a_k}
 {1+\varepsilon-a_kb_k}.
\end{equation}
On the other hand,
\[
 \int_0^\infty
 e^{-2\varepsilon b_k(t)\xi^2}\,d\xi
 =\frac{\sqrt\pi}{2\sqrt{2\varepsilon b_k(t)}}.
\]
Tonelli's theorem, \eqref{eq:stage-shifted}, and
\eqref{eq:half-line-lower} imply
\begin{equation}\label{eq:strict-next-bound}
 \int_{\{x_1>0\}}
 e^{2A_{k,\varepsilon}(t)x_1^2}|u(x,t)|^2\,dx
 \le C_*
 \left(\frac{1+\varepsilon-a_k(t)b_k(t)}
 {\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}.
\end{equation}
The right side is bounded uniformly in \(t\).  At \(t=\pm1\), where
\(b_k=0\), the exact endpoint \(e^{\mu x_{1,+}^2}u(\pm1)\in L^2\)
supplies the same conclusion.  The inactive half-space \(x_1\le0\) is
controlled by the unweighted \(L^2\) norm.

By \eqref{eq:positive-denominator},
\[
 A_{k,\varepsilon}\longrightarrow
 \frac{a_k}{1-a_kb_k}=a_{k+1}
\]
uniformly on \([-1,1]\) as \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\).  Given
\(0<\eta<\min a_{k+1}\), choose \(\varepsilon\) so small that
\[
 a_{k+1}(t)-\eta<A_{k,\varepsilon}(t)
 \quad\text{for every }t.
\]
Estimate \eqref{eq:strict-next-bound} proves
\eqref{eq:verified-stage-invariant} at stage \(k+1\).

If the recursion stops at a finite stage, the denominator argument already
proved \eqref{eq:iteration-conclusion}.  Suppose instead that it continues
indefinitely.  If \(\sup_ka_k(0)<\infty\), Lemma
\ref{lem:algebraic-recursion} implies \(\mu\le1/8\), contrary to the
hypothesis.  Hence \(a_k(0)\to\infty\).  For every \(\delta>0\),
\eqref{eq:uniform-exact-stage} gives
\[
 \int_{\{x_1>\delta\}}|u(x,0)|^2\,dx
 \le C_*e^{-2a_k(0)\delta^2}\longrightarrow0.
\]
Letting \(\delta\downarrow0\) proves
\eqref{eq:iteration-conclusion}.

nodes.title: Verified moving quadratic Carleman estimate
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(R>0\), let \(x_0\in\R\), let
\(\varphi\in C^2([0,1];\R)\), and put
\[
 z(x,t)=\frac{x_1-x_0}{R}+\varphi(t),\qquad
 M_1=\norm{\varphi'}_{L^\infty(0,1)},\qquad
 M_2=\norm{\varphi''}_{L^\infty(0,1)}.
\]
If
\[
 g\in C_c^\infty(\R^n\times(0,1)),\qquad
 \operatorname{supp}g\subset\{(x,t):|z(x,t)|\ge1\},
\]
and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:carleman-alpha-condition}
 \alpha>0,\qquad
 \frac{\alpha^2}{R^4}\ge\frac{M_2+3M_1^2}{4},
\end{equation}
then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:moving-quadratic-carleman}
 \frac{\alpha^{3/2}}{R^2}
 \norm{e^{\alpha z^2}g}_{L^2(\R^n\times(0,1))}
 \le\frac1{2\sqrt2}
 \norm{e^{\alpha z^2}(i\partial_t+\Delta)g}
 _{L^2(\R^n\times(0,1))}.
\end{equation}
Thus the lower threshold for \(\alpha/R^2\) depends only on
\(\norm{\varphi'}_\infty+\norm{\varphi''}_\infty\), and the constant in
\eqref{eq:moving-quadratic-carleman} is independent of \(R\), \(\alpha\),
\(x_0\), and \(g\).
nodes.proofBody:
All scalar products in this proof are those of
\(L^2(\R^n\times(0,1))\), linear in the first argument.  For a differential
operator \(B\), its formal adjoint \(B^*\) is defined by
\[
 \langle Bf,h\rangle=\langle f,B^*h\rangle
 \quad(f,h\in C_c^\infty(\R^n\times(0,1))).
\]
Set
\[
 f=e^{\alpha z^2}g.
\]
Direct differentiation, using
\[
 \nabla z^2=\frac{2z}{R}e_1,\qquad
 \Delta z^2=\frac2{R^2},\qquad
 \partial_tz^2=2\varphi'z,
\]
gives the exact conjugation identity
\begin{equation}\label{eq:carleman-conjugation}
 e^{\alpha z^2}(i\partial_t+\Delta)g
 =S_\alpha f-4\alpha A_\alpha f,
\end{equation}
where
\begin{align}
 S_\alpha
 &=i\partial_t+\Delta+\frac{4\alpha^2}{R^2}z^2,
 \label{eq:carleman-S}\\
 A_\alpha
 &=\frac zR\,\partial_{x_1}+\frac1{2R^2}
   +\frac{i\varphi'}2\,z.
 \label{eq:carleman-A}
\end{align}
Integration by parts in \(t\) and \(x\) has no boundary terms because
\(f\) is compactly supported in \(\R^n\times(0,1)\).  Consequently
\[
 (i\partial_t)^*=i\partial_t,\qquad
 \Delta^*=\Delta,\qquad
 \left(\frac zR\partial_{x_1}\right)^*
 =-\frac zR\partial_{x_1}-\frac1{R^2}.
\]
The coefficients \(z^2\), \(\varphi'\), and \(z\) are real.  Hence
\[
 S_\alpha^*=S_\alpha,\qquad A_\alpha^*=-A_\alpha.
\]
For the commutator convention
\([S_\alpha,A_\alpha]=S_\alpha A_\alpha-A_\alpha S_\alpha\), expansion of
the square in \eqref{eq:carleman-conjugation} therefore yields
\begin{equation}\label{eq:carleman-square}
 \norm{S_\alpha f-4\alpha A_\alpha f}_2^2
 \ge-4\alpha
 \langle[S_\alpha,A_\alpha]f,f\rangle.
\end{equation}
Indeed,
\[
 2\operatorname{Re}\langle S_\alpha f,A_\alpha f\rangle
 =\langle[S_\alpha,A_\alpha]f,f\rangle.
\]

We now compute every part of the commutator.  The elementary identities
\[
 [\Delta,z\partial_{x_1}]=\frac2R\partial_{x_1}^2,\qquad
 [z^2,z\partial_{x_1}]=-\frac2Rz^2,\qquad
 [\Delta,z]=\frac2R\partial_{x_1}
\]
follow by applying both sides to a test function.  Moreover,
\[
 [i\partial_t,R^{-1}z\partial_{x_1}]
 =\frac{i\varphi'}R\partial_{x_1},
\]
and, since multiplication operators commute,
\[
 \left[i\partial_t,\frac{i\varphi'}2z\right]
 =-\frac12(\varphi''z+(\varphi')^2).
\]
Finally,
\[
 \left[\Delta,\frac{i\varphi'}2z\right]
 =\frac{i\varphi'}R\partial_{x_1}.
\]
Substitution into \eqref{eq:carleman-S}--\eqref{eq:carleman-A} gives
\begin{equation}\label{eq:carleman-commutator}
 [S_\alpha,A_\alpha]
 =\frac2{R^2}\partial_{x_1}^2-\frac{8\alpha^2}{R^4}z^2
 -\frac12\bigl(\varphi''z+(\varphi')^2\bigr)
 +\frac{2i\varphi'}R\partial_{x_1}.
\end{equation}
The coefficient \(8\), rather than \(4\), in the second term is forced by
\[
 \left[\frac{4\alpha^2}{R^2}z^2,
       \frac zR\partial_{x_1}\right]
 =-\frac{8\alpha^2}{R^4}z^2.
\]

Using
\(\langle\partial_{x_1}^2f,f\rangle=-\norm{\partial_{x_1}f}_2^2\),
equations
\eqref{eq:carleman-square} and \eqref{eq:carleman-commutator} imply
\begin{align}
 &\norm{e^{\alpha z^2}(i\partial_t+\Delta)g}_2^2\notag\\
 &\quad\ge
 \frac{32\alpha^3}{R^4}\int z^2|f|^2
 +\frac{8\alpha}{R^2}\int|\partial_{x_1}f|^2
 +2\alpha\int\bigl(\varphi''z+(\varphi')^2\bigr)|f|^2
 -\frac{8\alpha i}{R}\int\varphi'\partial_{x_1}f\,\overline f.
\label{eq:carleman-positive-identity}
\end{align}
The last integral is real because it arose from a self-adjoint commutator;
only its absolute value is needed.  Cauchy--Schwarz and
\(2ab\le a^2+b^2\) give
\[
 \left|\frac{8\alpha i}{R}
 \int\varphi'\partial_{x_1}f\,\overline f\right|
 \le\frac{4\alpha}{R^2}\norm{\partial_{x_1}f}_2^2
 +4\alpha M_1^2\norm f_2^2.
\]
The remaining possibly negative term satisfies
\[
 2\alpha\int
 \bigl(\varphi''z+(\varphi')^2\bigr)|f|^2
 \ge
 -2\alpha M_2\int |z||f|^2
 -2\alpha M_1^2\norm f_2^2.
\]
On the support of \(f\), \(|z|\ge1\), so both
\(|z|\le z^2\) and \(1\le z^2\).  We conclude from
\eqref{eq:carleman-positive-identity} that
\begin{align*}
 \norm{e^{\alpha z^2}(i\partial_t+\Delta)g}_2^2
 &\ge
 \left(\frac{32\alpha^3}{R^4}
       -2\alpha M_2-6\alpha M_1^2\right)
 \int z^2|f|^2\\
 &\quad+\frac{4\alpha}{R^2}\norm{\partial_{x_1}f}_2^2.
\end{align*}
Condition \eqref{eq:carleman-alpha-condition} implies
\[
 2\alpha M_2+6\alpha M_1^2
 \le\frac{8\alpha^3}{R^4}.
\]
After discarding the nonnegative derivative term and part of the remaining
quadratic term, we obtain
\[
 \norm{e^{\alpha z^2}(i\partial_t+\Delta)g}_2^2
 \ge\frac{8\alpha^3}{R^4}
 \int z^2|f|^2
 \ge\frac{8\alpha^3}{R^4}\norm f_2^2.
\]
Taking square roots is exactly \eqref{eq:moving-quadratic-carleman}.

nodes.title: Verified quantitative right-strip elimination
nodes.proofBody:
We first record why Lemma \ref{lem:moving-quadratic-carleman} applies to
the nonsmooth cutoffs used below.  Suppose \(g\in L^2\),
\((i\partial_t+\Delta)g\in L^2\) distributionally, \(g\) has compact
support in the \((x_1,t)\)-variables, and
\[
 \operatorname{supp}g\subset\{|z|\ge1+\varepsilon\}
\]
for some \(\varepsilon>0\).  Take the partial Fourier transform in
\(x'=(x_2,\ldots,x_n)\).  At frequency \(\xi'\), the operator becomes
\[
 i\partial_t+\partial_{x_1}^2-|\xi'|^2.
\]
The real scalar \(-|\xi'|^2\) may be added to \(S_\alpha\); it commutes
with \(A_\alpha\), so every line in the proof of Lemma
\ref{lem:moving-quadratic-carleman} is unchanged.  Plancherel's theorem
therefore gives the same estimate for functions smooth and compactly
supported in \((x_1,t)\) and lying in the graph domain of
\(-\Delta_{x'}\).

For a general \(g\) as above, first apply
\[
 J_\nu=(1-\nu\Delta_{x'})^{-1}.
\]
This bounded Fourier multiplier commutes with
\(i\partial_t+\Delta\), with the weight, and with all
\((x_1,t)\)-cutoffs.  Next convolve in \((x_1,t)\) with a standard
mollifier of radius smaller than a fixed multiple of \(\varepsilon\).
Because \(z\) is Lipschitz on the compact support, the mollified function
is still supported in \(\{|z|\ge1\}\).  The mollified graph pair converges
in \(L^2\times L^2\), first as the mollifier radius tends to zero and then
as \(\nu\downarrow0\).  Lemma \ref{lem:moving-quadratic-carleman} and
Plancherel pass to the limit.  We shall use this graph-domain extension
without further comment.

Choose fixed functions
\[
 \rho\in C_c^\infty((1/32,31/32);[0,1]),\qquad
 \rho=1\quad\text{on }[3/8,5/8],
\]
and
\[
 \chi\in C^\infty(\R;[0,1]),\qquad
 \chi=0\ \text{on }(-\infty,1/2],\qquad
 \chi=1\ \text{on }[1,\infty).
\]
Also choose a nondecreasing
\(\eta\in C^\infty(\R;[0,1])\) satisfying
\[
 \eta=0\quad\text{on }(-\infty,1/4],\qquad
 \eta=1\quad\text{on }[1,\infty).
\]
For \(R\ge8\), put
\begin{align*}
 \varphi_R(t)&=\left(\frac32-\frac1R\right)\rho(t),\\
 z_R(x,t)&=\frac{x_1-R/2}{R}+\varphi_R(t),\\
 \theta_R(x_1)&=\chi(x_1)\chi(R-x_1),\\
 \eta_R(s)&=\eta\bigl(2R(s-1)\bigr).
\end{align*}
Then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:eta-R-properties}
 \eta_R=0\quad\text{on }(-\infty,1+1/(8R)],\qquad
 \eta_R=1\quad\text{on }[1+1/(2R),\infty),
\end{equation}
and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:eta-R-derivatives}
 \norm{\eta_R'}_\infty\le C_\eta R,\qquad
 \norm{\eta_R''}_\infty\le C_\eta R^2.
\end{equation}
The quantities
\[
 M_1=\sup_R\norm{\varphi_R'}_\infty,\qquad
 M_2=\sup_R\norm{\varphi_R''}_\infty
\]
are finite.  Fix once and for all \(\kappa\ge1\) such that
\begin{equation}\label{eq:kappa-choice}
 \kappa^2\ge\frac{M_2+3M_1^2}{4},
\end{equation}
and define
\begin{equation}\label{eq:alpha-kappa-R}
 \alpha=\kappa R^2.
\end{equation}
Thus \eqref{eq:carleman-alpha-condition} holds for every \(R\ge8\).
At the end of the proof we take
\begin{equation}\label{eq:Lambda-choice}
 \Lambda=3\kappa.
\end{equation}

We next prove that the mass in the fixed slab vanishes.  Set
\begin{equation}\label{eq:central-mass}
 B^2=\int_{3/8}^{5/8}\int_{\{2<x_1<3\}}|u(x,t)|^2\,dx\,dt.
\end{equation}
Suppose, for contradiction, that \(B>0\).  Define
\begin{equation}\label{eq:carleman-cutoff-g}
 g_R(x,t)=\theta_R(x_1)\eta_R(z_R(x,t))u(x,t).
\end{equation}
The spatial cutoff confines \(g_R\) to \(1/2<x_1<R-1/2\).  If
\(\rho(t)=0\), then \(z_R<1/2\) there, so \(\eta_R(z_R)=0\).
Consequently \(g_R\) has compact support in the \((x_1,t)\)-variables.
By \eqref{eq:eta-R-properties},
\begin{equation}\label{eq:g-support-away}
 \operatorname{supp}g_R
 \subset\{z_R\ge1+1/(8R)\}.
\end{equation}

On \(\{2<x_1<3\}\times[3/8,5/8]\), one has
\[
 \theta_R=1,\qquad
 z_R=1+\frac{x_1-1}{R}\ge1+\frac1R,
 \qquad \eta_R(z_R)=1.
\]
Hence \(g_R=u\) there and
\begin{equation}\label{eq:carleman-left-lower}
 \norm{e^{\alpha z_R^2}g_R}_2
 \ge B e^{\alpha(1+1/R)^2}.
\end{equation}

Our sign convention gives
\[
 (i\partial_t+\Delta)u=-Vu.
\]
In particular, \(i\partial_t-\Delta\) would not reduce to \(-V\) under
the displayed evolution equation; the plus sign is essential.
For \(h_R=\theta_R\eta_R(z_R)\), the complete product rule is
\begin{align}
 (i\partial_t+\Delta)(h_Ru)
 ={}&-Vh_Ru\notag\\
 &+\eta_R(z_R)
 \bigl(2\theta_R'\partial_{x_1}u+\theta_R''u\bigr)\notag\\
 &+\frac{2\theta_R\eta_R'(z_R)}R\partial_{x_1}u\notag\\
 &+\theta_R\left(i\eta_R'(z_R)\varphi_R'
       +\frac{\eta_R''(z_R)}{R^2}\right)u\notag\\
 &+\frac{2\theta_R'\eta_R'(z_R)}R\,u.
\label{eq:complete-cutoff-expansion}
\end{align}
In particular, the two terms containing
\(\eta_R'\partial_{x_1}u\) and \(\theta_R'\eta_R'\) cannot be omitted.
Assumption \eqref{eq:right-strip-hypothesis} and
\eqref{eq:eta-R-derivatives} show that every term in
\eqref{eq:complete-cutoff-expansion} belongs to \(L^2\), so the graph
extension of the Carleman estimate applies to \(g_R\).

The potential term satisfies
\[
 \norm{e^{\alpha z_R^2}Vg_R}_2
 \le\norm V_\infty\norm{e^{\alpha z_R^2}g_R}_2.
\]
Since
\[
 \frac{\alpha^{3/2}}{R^2}=\kappa^{3/2}R,
\]
we may take \(R\), depending here on \(\norm V_\infty\), so large that
this term is absorbed into one half of the left side of
\eqref{eq:moving-quadratic-carleman}.  This lower bound on \(R\) does not
alter the fixed value of \(\kappa\), which is why the final Gaussian
threshold is independent of the size of \(V\).

The derivatives of \(\theta_R\) are supported where either
\[
 \frac12<x_1<1
 \quad\text{or}\quad
 R-1<x_1<R-\frac12.
\]
On the first region,
\[
 z_R\le\frac1R-\frac12+\frac32-\frac1R=1,
\]
so \(\eta_R(z_R)=0\), including all its derivatives.  On the second
region,
\[
 z_R\le2-\frac1R.
\]
Define
\begin{equation}\label{eq:delta-R}
 \delta(R)=\int_{1/32}^{31/32}
 \int_{\{R-1<x_1<R\}}
 \bigl(|u|^2+|\partial_{x_1}u|^2\bigr)\,dx\,dt.
\end{equation}
The terms carrying a derivative of \(\theta_R\), except those already
placed in the final term of \eqref{eq:complete-cutoff-expansion}, are
therefore bounded in weighted \(L^2\) by
\begin{equation}\label{eq:theta-error}
 C_\chi e^{\alpha(2-1/R)^2}\delta(R)^{1/2}.
\end{equation}
The final term has the same bound when
\(\eta_R'\) meets the right transition of \(\theta_R\).

It remains to estimate the terms carrying a derivative of \(\eta_R\)
away from that right transition.  Their support is contained in
\[
 1+\frac1{8R}\le z_R\le1+\frac1{2R}.
\]
Since \(\varphi_R\le3/2-1/R\), this support also satisfies
\[
 x_1=R\left(z_R+\frac12-\varphi_R\right)\ge\frac98>1.
\]
Using \eqref{eq:eta-R-derivatives} and \(R\ge1\), their weighted norm is
at most
\begin{equation}\label{eq:eta-error}
 C_{\rho,\eta,\chi}RK_\sigma
 e^{\alpha(1+1/(2R))^2}.
\end{equation}
This estimate includes the previously omitted term
\(2R^{-1}\eta_R'\partial_{x_1}u\).

Combining \eqref{eq:moving-quadratic-carleman},
\eqref{eq:carleman-left-lower}, and
\eqref{eq:theta-error}--\eqref{eq:eta-error}, after potential absorption,
gives
\begin{align}
 c_0\kappa^{3/2}RB e^{\alpha(1+1/R)^2}
 \le{}&
 C_0e^{\alpha(2-1/R)^2}\delta(R)^{1/2}\notag\\
 &+C_1RK_\sigma
 e^{\alpha(1+1/(2R))^2}.
\label{eq:lower-bound-before-gap}
\end{align}
Here and below the positive constants \(c_0,C_0,C_1\) depend only on the
three fixed cutoff profiles.

The exponential gap between the first and last terms is
\begin{align*}
 \alpha\left[
 \left(1+\frac1R\right)^2
 -\left(1+\frac1{2R}\right)^2\right]
 &=\kappa R^2\left(\frac1R+\frac3{4R^2}\right)\\
 &=\kappa R+\frac{3\kappa}{4}.
\end{align*}
Because \(B>0\), the last term in
\eqref{eq:lower-bound-before-gap} is absorbed by its left side for all
sufficiently large \(R\), now allowed to depend on \(B\) and \(K_\sigma\).
Thus
\[
 c_2RB e^{\alpha(1+1/R)^2}
 \le C_0e^{\alpha(2-1/R)^2}\delta(R)^{1/2}.
\]
Since
\[
 \left(2-\frac1R\right)^2
 -\left(1+\frac1R\right)^2
 =3-\frac6R,
\]
we obtain, for all sufficiently large \(R\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:delta-lower}
 \delta(R)
 \ge c_B R^2e^{-6\kappa R^2+12\kappa R}
 \ge c_Be^{-6\kappa R^2},
\end{equation}
where \(c_B>0\) may depend on the particular solution but not on \(R\).

On the other hand, \eqref{eq:right-strip-hypothesis} immediately gives
\begin{equation}\label{eq:delta-upper}
 \delta(R)\le K_\sigma^2e^{-2\sigma(R-1)^2}.
\end{equation}
If \(\sigma>3\kappa=\Lambda\), then
\[
 2\sigma(R-1)^2-6\kappa R^2
 =2(\sigma-3\kappa)R^2-4\sigma R+2\sigma
 \longrightarrow+\infty.
\]
The upper bound \eqref{eq:delta-upper} is therefore eventually strictly
smaller than the lower bound \eqref{eq:delta-lower}, a contradiction.
Thus \(B=0\).

Finally, for every integer \(m\ge0\), apply the argument to
\[
 u_m(y,t)=u(y_1+m,y',t),\qquad
 V_m(y,t)=V(y_1+m,y',t).
\]
For \(y_1>1\),
\[
 e^{2\sigma y_1^2}\le e^{2\sigma(y_1+m)^2},
\]
so \eqref{eq:right-strip-hypothesis} for \(u_m\) follows from that for
\(u\), with no increase in the threshold \(\Lambda\).  Hence \(u=0\) on
each strip
\[
 \{m+2<x_1<m+3\}\times(3/8,5/8).
\]
Their union over \(m\ge0\) is
\(\{x_1>2\}\times(3/8,5/8)\), proving
\eqref{eq:right-strip-conclusion}.

nodes.title: Verified lateral propagation across a hyperplane
nodes.proofBody:
Choose an intermediate open interval \(J''\) with
\[
 J'\Subset J''\Subset J.
\]
There are real functions
\(\tau\in C_c^\infty(J)\) and \(\varphi\in C^\infty(J)\), with
\begin{align*}
 &0\le\tau\le1,\qquad \tau=1\ \text{on }J'',\\
 &4\le\varphi\le6,\qquad
 \varphi=6\ \text{on }J'',\qquad
 \varphi=4\ \text{on }\operatorname{supp}\tau'.
\end{align*}
More explicitly, first choose \(\tau\) equal to one on \(J''\) and
supported in \(J\); then choose \(\varphi=6\) on \(J''\), decrease it
smoothly to \(4\) before reaching \(\operatorname{supp}\tau'\), and keep it
equal to \(4\) there.  Only \(\tau\) must be compactly supported;
\(\varphi\) may be extended smoothly to all of \(J\).

Let \(\chi\in C^\infty(\R;[0,1])\) satisfy
\[
 \chi=0\quad\text{on }(-\infty,-2],\qquad
 \chi=1\quad\text{on }[-1,\infty),
\]
and put
\[
 y=\frac{x_1-a}{R},\qquad
 z(x,t)=y+\varphi(t),\qquad
 g(x,t)=\chi(y)\tau(t)u(x,t).
\]
On \(\operatorname{supp}g\), condition
\eqref{eq:lateral-initial-zero} gives \(y\le0\), while \(\chi(y)\ne0\)
gives \(y\ge-2\).  Thus \(z\ge2\), and \(g\) has compact support in the
\((x_1,t)\)-variables.  Its complete equation is
\begin{align}
 (i\partial_t+\Delta)g
 ={}&-Vg+i\chi(y)\tau'(t)u\notag\\
 &+\tau(t)\left(
 \frac{2\chi'(y)}R\partial_{x_1}u
 +\frac{\chi''(y)}{R^2}u\right).
\label{eq:lateral-cutoff-equation}
\end{align}
Every term lies in \(L^2\) by the assumed local derivative regularity.
The graph-domain extension proved at the beginning of Theorem
