\ref{thm:right-strip-elimination} therefore permits application of Lemma
\ref{lem:moving-quadratic-carleman}.  Although that lemma was written on
\((0,1)\), its proof uses only integrations by parts for a compactly
time-supported function and therefore applies verbatim on any open time
interval containing \(\operatorname{supp}\tau\).

Take \(\alpha\to\infty\), subject to
\eqref{eq:carleman-alpha-condition}.  The potential contribution is
absorbed because
\[
 \frac{\alpha^{3/2}}{R^2}\longrightarrow\infty.
\]
On the target region
\[
 \{a-R/2<x_1<a\}\times J',
\]
one has \(\chi=\tau=1\), \(\varphi=6\), and
\[
 z\ge6-\frac12=\frac{11}{2}.
\]
If the \(L^2\) norm of \(u\) on this target region were \(D>0\), the
Carleman left side would be at least
\begin{equation}\label{eq:lateral-target-lower}
 \frac{\alpha^{3/2}}{R^2}D e^{(121/4)\alpha}.
\end{equation}

The spatial cutoff errors in \eqref{eq:lateral-cutoff-equation} are
supported where \(-2<y<-1\).  Since \(\varphi\le6\), they have
\[
 2<z<5.
\]
Their weighted norm is bounded by
\[
 C_R e^{25\alpha}
 \norm{(|u|+|\partial_{x_1}u|)
 \one_{\{a-2R<x_1<a-R\}\times\operatorname{supp}\tau}}_2.
\]
The temporal cutoff error is supported where \(\varphi=4\); there
\(-2<y\le0\), and hence \(2<z\le4\).  Its weighted norm is bounded by
\[
 C_\tau e^{16\alpha}
 \norm{u\one_{\{a-2R<x_1<a\}\times\operatorname{supp}\tau'}}_2.
\]
All unweighted norms in these two displays are finite.  Dividing the
Carleman inequality by \(e^{(121/4)\alpha}\) and letting
\(\alpha\to\infty\) forces \(D=0\), because
\[
 25<\frac{121}{4},\qquad 16<\frac{121}{4}.
\]
Together with \eqref{eq:lateral-initial-zero}, this proves
\eqref{eq:lateral-propagated-zero}.

nodes.title: Verified equal-rate full-space elimination
nodes.statementBody:
There is a numerical constant
\[
 \Gamma=\Lambda<\infty
\]
such that the following holds in every dimension.  Let
\(v\in C([0,1];L^2(\R^n))\) be a Duhamel solution of
\[
 \partial_tv=i(\Delta v+Vv),
\]
where \(V\in L^\infty(\R^n\times[0,1])\) has integrably vanishing right
tail.  If
\[
 e^{\gamma x_{1,+}^2}v(0),\qquad
 e^{\gamma x_{1,+}^2}v(1)\in L^2(\R^n),
 \qquad \gamma>\Gamma,
\]
then \(v\equiv0\).
nodes.proofBody:
Choose
\[
 \Lambda<\sigma<\gamma.
\]
We first convert Theorem \ref{thm:stationary} from \([-1,1]\) to
\([0,1]\).  Set
\[
 U(y,s)=2^{-n/4}v(y/\sqrt2,(s+1)/2),\qquad -1\le s\le1.
\]
Then
\[
 \partial_sU=i\left(\Delta_yU+
 \frac12V(y/\sqrt2,(s+1)/2)U\right),
\]
and the common endpoint exponent in the \(y_1\)-variable is
\(\gamma/2\).  Apply Theorem \ref{thm:stationary} with
\[
 a=\frac{\sigma}{2}<\frac{\gamma}{2}.
\]
Returning to \((x,t)\), and using that the convex function \(q\) in that
theorem equals \(r^2\) for \(r\ge1\), gives
\begin{align}
 &\sup_{0\le t\le1}\int_{\{x_1>1\}}
 e^{2\sigma x_1^2}|v(x,t)|^2\,dx\notag\\
 &\quad+
 \int_{1/32}^{31/32}\int_{\{x_1>1\}}
 e^{2\sigma x_1^2}
 \bigl(|v|^2+|\partial_{x_1}v|^2\bigr)\,dx\,dt<\infty.
\label{eq:equal-rate-stationary-consequence}
\end{align}
Changing the harmless fixed boundary \(x_1>1/\sqrt2\) produced by the
scaling to \(x_1>1\) only restricts the integration region.
Theorem \ref{thm:right-strip-elimination} now yields
\begin{equation}\label{eq:equal-rate-open-zero}
 v=0\quad\text{on }\{x_1>2\}\times(3/8,5/8).
\end{equation}

We next verify the local derivative hypothesis needed for Lemma
\ref{lem:lateral-propagation} at every finite spatial position, not only
on the right.  Fix \(A\in\R\) and choose
\[
 0<\sigma_A<\gamma.
\]
For \(y_1\in\R\), the continuous function
\[
 \sigma_A y_{1,+}^2-\gamma(y_1+A)_+^2
\]
is bounded above: it is bounded on every finite interval and tends to
\(-\infty\) as \(y_1\to+\infty\), because
\(\sigma_A<\gamma\).  Thus there is \(C_A<\infty\) such that
\begin{equation}\label{eq:shifted-endpoint-domination}
 \sigma_Ay_{1,+}^2
 \le\gamma(y_1+A)_+^2+C_A.
\end{equation}
Apply Theorem \ref{thm:stationary}, after the same time rescaling and with
any positive output exponent strictly below \(\sigma_A/2\), to
\[
 v_A(y,t)=v(y_1+A,y',t).
\]
The translated potential still has integrably vanishing right tail, and
\eqref{eq:shifted-endpoint-domination} supplies its endpoint Gaussian
hypothesis.  The graph conclusion of that theorem shows that \(v\)
satisfies \eqref{eq:slab-local-derivative} on every compact time interval
in \((0,1)\), because \(A\) can be chosen arbitrarily far to the left of
any prescribed bounded \(x_1\)-interval, while the estimate always
integrates over the whole transverse space \(\R^{n-1}\).

It remains to propagate \eqref{eq:equal-rate-open-zero}.  Fix
\(N\in\mathbb N\).  Choose nested open intervals
\[
 (3/8,5/8)=J_0\Supset J_1\Supset\cdots\Supset J_N,
\qquad \frac12\in J_N,\qquad J_{j+1}\Subset J_j.
\]
Starting with \(a_0=2\), apply Lemma
\ref{lem:lateral-propagation} successively with \(R=1\).  After \(N\)
steps,
\[
 v=0\quad\text{on }\{x_1>2-N/2\}\times J_N.
\]
Since \(v\) is continuous into \(L^2\), this vanishing holds at
\(t=1/2\).  The half-spaces
\[
 \{x_1>2-N/2\},\qquad N=1,2,\ldots,
\]
increase to \(\R^n\).  Hence monotone convergence gives
\[
 \norm{v(1/2)}_2^2
 =\lim_{N\to\infty}
 \int_{\{x_1>2-N/2\}}|v(x,1/2)|^2\,dx
 =0.
\]

Finally, the Duhamel identity and unitarity of the free Schr\"odinger
group imply, for \(t\ge1/2\),
\[
 \norm{v(t)}_2
 \le\norm V_\infty\int_{1/2}^t\norm{v(r)}_2\,dr.
\]
Gronwall's inequality gives \(v(t)=0\) on \([1/2,1]\).  For
\(0\le t\le1/2\), the backward Duhamel formula gives
\[
 \norm{v(t)}_2
 \le\norm V_\infty\int_t^{1/2}\norm{v(r)}_2\,dr.
\]
The backward form of Gronwall's inequality gives \(v(t)=0\) on
\([0,1/2]\).  Hence \(v\equiv0\).

nodes.title: Exact product-form reduction
nodes.statementBody:
Let \(s_0<s_1\), let \(L=s_1-s_0\), and let \(v\) be a full-space Duhamel
solution on \([s_0,s_1]\) whose bounded potential has integrably vanishing
right tail.  If
\[
 e^{Ax_{1,+}^2}v(s_0),\qquad
 e^{Bx_{1,+}^2}v(s_1)\in L^2(\R^n)
\]
for \(A,B>0\), then
\begin{equation}\label{eq:product-halfspace}
 L^2AB>\Gamma^2\quad\Longrightarrow\quad v\equiv0.
\end{equation}
In particular, the product threshold is numerical and dimension
independent.
nodes.proofBody:
Translate \(s_0\) to zero and apply the unitary parabolic scaling
\[
 U(y,\tau)=L^{n/4}v(L^{1/2}y,s_0+L\tau),
 \qquad 0\le\tau\le1.
\]
The endpoint norm exponents become \(LA\) and \(LB\).  Put
\[
 \beta=(LA)^{-1/2},\qquad
 \alpha=(LB)^{-1/2}.
\]
Apply the Appell transform \eqref{eq:appell}, now without the final
conversion from \([0,1]\) to \([-1,1]\).  The norm identity
\eqref{eq:weight-identity} shows that the common endpoint exponent is
\[
 \gamma=\frac1{\alpha\beta}=L\sqrt{AB}.
\]
All spatial changes in the \(x_1\)-variable are multiplications by positive
numbers, so \(\{x_1>0\}\) and \(x_{1,+}\) are preserved.  The tail
calculation in Lemma \ref{lem:appell} applies verbatim and shows that the
new potential remains bounded with integrably vanishing right tail.
Therefore Theorem \ref{thm:equal-rate-elimination} gives vanishing whenever
\[
 L\sqrt{AB}>\Gamma,
\]
which is exactly \eqref{eq:product-halfspace}.  The transformations are
invertible, so the original \(v\) vanishes.

nodes.title: Explicit endpoint counterexample
nodes.statementBody:
For every \(n\ge1\), the constant \(1/16\) in
\eqref{eq:threshold} cannot be replaced by a smaller constant under the
hypotheses of Theorem \ref{thm:conditional}.  More precisely, whenever
\[
 T^2c_0c_1=\frac1{16},
\]
there are a nonzero smooth solution and a smooth bounded complex potential
that satisfy \eqref{eq:tail-mod-scalar} with \(c=0\) and the endpoint
conditions \eqref{eq:endpoint-data}.
nodes.proofBody:
We first construct the normalized example on \([-1,1]\).  Let
\(k>n/2\), put \(z=1+it\), \(s=|x|^2\), and define
\begin{equation}\label{eq:counterexample-u}
 u_k(x,t)=
 z^{\,2k-n/2}(1+s)^{-k}
 \exp\left(-\frac{s}{4z}\right).
\end{equation}
The complex power is defined using the smooth branch of \(\log z\) on
\(\{1+it:-1\le t\le1\}\), which does not meet the origin.  Define
\begin{equation}\label{eq:counterexample-V}
 V_k(x,t)=\frac1{1+s}
 \left(
 \frac{2k}{z}+2kn-\frac{4k(k+1)s}{1+s}
 \right).
\end{equation}
Both functions are smooth, and \(u_k\) is nonzero.

We verify the equation rather than cite it.  Write \(u_k=e^L\), where
\[
 L=(2k-n/2)\log z-k\log(1+s)-\frac{s}{4z}.
\]
Then
\[
 \partial_tL=\frac{i(2k-n/2)}z+\frac{is}{4z^2}.
\]
If
\[
 g(s)=-k\log(1+s)-\frac{s}{4z},
\]
then
\[
 g'(s)=-\frac{k}{1+s}-\frac1{4z},
 \qquad
 g''(s)=\frac{k}{(1+s)^2},
\]
and the radial identity
\[
 \frac{\Delta e^{g(|x|^2)}}{e^{g(|x|^2)}}
 =4s\bigl(g''(s)+g'(s)^2\bigr)+2ng'(s)
\]
gives
\[
 -i\partial_tL-\frac{\Delta u_k}{u_k}
 =
 \frac1{1+s}
 \left(
 \frac{2k}{z}+2kn-\frac{4k(k+1)s}{1+s}
 \right)
 =V_k.
\]
Therefore
\[
 \partial_tu_k=i(\Delta u_k+V_ku_k).
\]

Since \(|z|\) is bounded above and below on \([-1,1]\), formula
\eqref{eq:counterexample-V} implies
\[
 |V_k(x,t)|\le\frac{C_{n,k}}{1+|x|^2}.
\]
Thus \(V_k\) is bounded and
\[
 \int_{-1}^1
 \norm{V_k(\cdot,t)}_{L^\infty(\{x_1>R\})}\,dt
 \le\frac{2C_{n,k}}{1+R^2}\longrightarrow0.
\]
At \(t=\pm1\),
\[
 \left|\exp\left(-\frac{|x|^2}{4(1\pm i)}\right)\right|
 =e^{-|x|^2/8}.
\]
Consequently
\[
 e^{|x|^2/8}|u_k(x,\pm1)|
 =2^{\,k-n/4}(1+|x|^2)^{-k}\in L^2(\R^n),
\]
where \(k>n/2\) is more than sufficient.  In particular,
\[
 e^{x_{1,+}^2/8}u_k(\pm1)\in L^2(\R^n).
\]
The interval has length \(2\), and both one-sided endpoint exponents equal
\(1/8\), so
\[
 2^2\left(\frac18\right)^2=\frac1{16}.
\]

For arbitrary \(T,c_0,c_1>0\) with \(T^2c_0c_1=1/16\), apply the inverse
of the transformations in Lemma \ref{lem:appell}, followed by the inverse
time translation.  Those maps are smooth and invertible, preserve
nontriviality, and preserve boundedness and integrably vanishing right
tails by the calculation in Lemma \ref{lem:appell}.  Their endpoint norm
identity is \eqref{eq:weight-identity}, read in reverse, and therefore
produces exactly the prescribed exponents \(c_0,c_1\).

nodes.title: Audit of the supplied proof
nodes.statementBody:
The supplied file \path{one-sided-hardy-unique-continuation.tex} does not
by itself contain a self-contained proof of the unconditional conclusion
in Theorem \ref{thm:conditional}.  The independent arguments in the
present file discharge every missing obligation and prove that conclusion.
nodes.proofBody:
The gauge and Appell reductions are proved directly above, and
Proposition \ref{prop:endpoint} independently verifies endpoint sharpness.
The original chain had three logically separate obligations.

First, the stationary propagation step invokes a uniform linear
exponential estimate and then asserts a weighted graph-closure argument
for merely bounded measurable potentials.  Lemma
\ref{lem:graph-closure} now supplies the missing strict-loss domination,
including the weighted potential commutator and all cutoff errors.  The
Fourier projection and regularizing-multiplier commutators required by the
linear estimate are proved directly in Lemma \ref{lem:fourier-transport},
including the signs of the positive- and negative-frequency transport terms.
Lemmas \ref{lem:linear-exponential} and \ref{lem:tail-exponential} insert
those estimates into the two-endpoint energy argument, pass through the
bounded exponential truncations, and perform the right-tail potential split.
Lemma \ref{lem:SA-logconv} independently proves the abstract logarithmic
convexity step, including the completion of squares and the bounded complex
potential residual.  Theorem \ref{thm:stationary} combines these components,
constructs and truncates the convex quadratic weight, proves a truncation-
radius-independent estimate, and retains the positive commutator terms.
Thus obligation \textup{(P)} is now fully discharged.

Second, the sharp iteration imports the algebraic recursion and several
sign and compactness identities from Section 3 of
Escauriaza--Kenig--Ponce--Vega \cite{EKPV2010}.  It also uses a new
Gaussian-scale one-sided commutator identity and claims that its
stage-dependent errors are uniformly summable.  For a self-contained
proof, all of these steps must be derived in the present one-sided setting.
Lemma \ref{lem:algebraic-recursion} now verifies the recursion, preservation
of its structural inequalities, the weighted integral identity, compactness
of a hypothetical bounded iteration, and the exact origin of the threshold
\(1/8\).  Lemma \ref{lem:commutator} verifies the underlying fourth-order
commutator formula.  Lemma \ref{lem:weighted-SA-logconv} proves the
weighted-time logarithmic-convexity principle needed by the iteration.
Lemma \ref{lem:moving-gaussian} proves the moving one-sided Gaussian
completion and the comparison between its Green-function loss \(T\) and
the shift parameter \(b\).  Proposition \ref{prop:shifted-gaussian}
justifies the analytic shifted-weight estimate for Duhamel solutions and
shows that its constant depends on the stage only through
\(\int_0^1a\).  Theorem \ref{thm:verified-iteration} proves a uniform
bound for that integral, carries out the positive-half-line parameter
integration with its strict loss, treats the finite denominator
obstruction, and closes the infinite recursion.  Thus obligation
\textup{(I)} is fully discharged.

Third, Theorem 11 of \cite{EKPV2012} is originally stated for a half-space
solution with an additional local \(L^2\) assumption on
\(\partial_{x_1}u\), and with two endpoint constants described only as
sufficiently large.  Remark (a) following that theorem explains how the
local derivative assumption can be recovered for a full-space
\(C_tL_x^2\) solution, but doing so uses another weighted Gaussian and
gradient estimate.  Lemma \ref{lem:product-halfspace} now verifies the
product-form reduction and the exact relation
\(C=\Gamma^2\).  Lemma
\ref{lem:moving-quadratic-carleman} independently proves the quadratic
moving-weight Carleman estimate used in the lower-bound argument, including
the full conjugation formula, the formal-adjoint signs, every commutator
term, and an explicit absorption threshold.  Theorem
\ref{thm:right-strip-elimination} extends that estimate to the required
graph domain, recomputes every cutoff derivative, retains the two terms
missing from formula (5.17) of \cite{EKPV2012}, corrects the operator sign
in that formula, and proves the quantitative lower/upper tail contradiction
with a Gaussian threshold independent of the potential size.  Lemma
\ref{lem:lateral-propagation} proves propagation across each spatial
hyperplane directly from the same Carleman estimate.  Theorem
\ref{thm:equal-rate-elimination} obtains the required local derivative
regularity from translated copies of the stationary estimate, iterates
the lateral propagation, and finishes by Duhamel uniqueness.  Thus
obligation \textup{(E)} is fully discharged.
